Seeing and Being Seen: Expanding Award Access for Women and Racial Minorities (Session ID 949)
speakers: Ny Mia Tran | Angela Grotto | Julie Olson-Buchanan | Kisha Jones | Mateo Cruz, Ph.D. (he/him)
speaker_affiliations: Salem State University | Montclair State University | California State University, Fresno | Florida International University - College of Business | Bentley University
speaker_titles: Associate Professor of Human Resource Analytics | Dean, Craig School of Business & Sid Craig Dean’s Chair | Assistant Professor of Management
description: Gadles, M. (Chair), Cruz, M., Grotto, A. R., Jones, K. S., & Olson-Buchanan, J. B. (2026, April–May). Seeing and being seen: Expanding award access for women and racial minorities [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Awards are career resources that open doors to collaborators, funding, and visibility—yet women and racially/ethnically minoritized colleagues remain underrecognized, and recipients can face backlash or devaluation after a win. This session will share real stories of what helped and what didn’t, point out where the process can feel unfair, and offer practical tools, such as how to apply, how to ask for strong letters, and how to use recognition to grow your career.

title: Moving Beyond Benchmarks: The Role of I-O Psychology Expertise in Total Rewards (Session ID 1271)
speakers: George Montgomery | Alec Munc | Nathan Bjornberg | Timothy Willis | Taylor Scotese
speaker_affiliations: Willis Towers Watson | Eli Lilly | Meta | Humana | WTW
speaker_titles: Employee Experience Texas Market & LATAM Regional Leader (Senior Director) | People Analytics | Compensation Analytics Lead | Director, Employee Experience
description: Bjornberg, N., Montgomery, G., Munc, A., Myer, A., Scotese, T., Willis, T.. (2026). Moving Beyond Benchmarks: The Role of I-O Psychology Expertise in Total Rewards (Compensation and Benefits). Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session will explore the significance of I-Os to the field of Total Rewards (TR). Panelists will share their applied and theoretical perspectives on best practices, challenges, and the unique positioning of I-Os in this field. Points will center around defining TR, the value I-Os can bring to these roles, the power of data-driven strategies, the impact of analytics technologies, and managing stakeholder resistance to change. The session aims to highlight the value of I-O psychology in enhancing TR strategies and opportunities for influence within this area.

title: “The Past Is Prologue:” Studying Old Leadership Lessons Using New Behavioral Methods (Session ID 1087)
speakers: Marcus Dickson | Andrew Loignon | Nissa Gongora | Richard Guzzo
speaker_affiliations: Wayne State University | Center for Creative Leadership | Workforce Sciences Institute

description: Dickson, M. W. (Co-Chair) & Jones, T. R. (Co-Chair) (2026, April 30 – May 2). The Past is Prologue: Studying Old Leadership Lessons Using New Behavioral Methods. [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Ancient and recent lessons about the importance of behavioral leadership assessment have set the stage for updated ways of assessing leadership. New behavioral simulations, AI-driven assessment of critical thinking, organic organizational data, and the use of an escape room setting all provide behavior-based insights on leadership and leadership emergence.

title: Blending Kirkpatrick and Psychometrics to Evaluate Leadership Development Impact (Session ID 577)
speakers: Stewart Desson
speaker_affiliations: Lumina Learning
speaker_titles: Association for Business Psychology Treasurer & CEO Lumina Learning
description: Desson, S., & Cannon, J. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Blending Kirkpatrick and Psychometrics to Evaluate Leadership Development Impact. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Evaluating leadership development needs more than ROI metrics. Two large-scale programs with Lumina Learning used an integrated framework combining psychometrics, experiential learning, and Kirkpatrick’s model. Among 72 leaders, results showed significant gains in reflective communication (+16%), resilience (+14%), and purposeful argumentation (+6%), alongside stronger inclusion and psychological safety. This multimethod, human-centered approach captures the true complexity of leadership impact beyond linear or financial measures.

title: Future-Proofing Selection Tools for Diverse Minds and Emerging AI (Session ID 649)
speakers: Ana Garcia | Viena Cao
speaker_affiliations: Cappfinity
speaker_titles: Consultant | Senior Analyst, Assessment Development & Talent Science
description: Cao, V., Rowe, E.-L., Garcia, A., Mueller, S., Dovey, H., Deakin, P. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Future-Proofing Selection Tools for Diverse Minds and Emerging AI [Master Tutorial]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This intermediate-level session explores the dual challenge facing talent assessment today of ensuring fairness for neurodiverse candidates while maintaining assessment integrity in the age of GenAI. This tutorial explores how inclusive design and AI resilience can be addressed together, not in conflict. Featuring a real-world case study from Cappfinity, attendees will gain insights into reimagining traditional assessments such as situational judgement tests (SJTs) to be more secure, inclusive, and future-ready, without compromising psychometric rigor.

title: Community of Interest: Bricolage in I-O: How to Do More With Less in a Changing World (Session ID 1432)
speakers: Jonathan Kochert | Matthew Christensen | Roza Jankovic | Kyle Benbow
speaker_affiliations: U.S. Army Research Institute for Behavioral and Social Sciences | Deloitte Consulting | Culture Amp | Army Research Institute

description: Benbow, K. L., Christensen, M., Jankovic, R., & Kochert, J. (2026, Month TBD, Day TBD) Community of Interest: Bricolage in IO: How to do more with less in a changing world. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Pressures from emerging trends are forcing organizations to do more with less. Are I-Os addressing these demands in a purposeful manner? The concept of bricolage provides a framework for addressing these trends. How can the systems and personnel characteristics that will lead to success in this environment be identified? How can the “do more with less mentality” be discarded and this as an opportunity to be adaptive, creative, and playful be embraced? This will be an audience participation session; presenters are eager to hear how attendees are/would respond to these trends.

Rethinking Generational Differences in the Age of AI: Stereotypes, Change, and the Future of Work [Panel]. Society for Industrial-Organizational 2026 annual conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. As AI transforms the workplace, it is also altering perspective on generational differences. This panel brings together researchers and practitioners for a conversation about how AI may reinforce or challenge generational stereotypes and explore new frameworks of workforce diversity in an AI-driven world. title: Creative Micropractices: Cultivating Reflection, Connection, and Workplace Well-Being (Session ID1069)
speakers: Patricia Friberg
speaker_affiliations: Patricia Friberg Coaching
speaker_titles: Applied Positive Psychology & Coaching Psychology, Art Therapist, Doctoral Cand.
description: Friberg, Patricia (2026, April 30-May 2). Creative Micro-Practices: Cultivating Reflection, Connection, & Workplace Well-being {Master Tutoria} Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States Relational well-being is recognized as essential for workplace well-being and overall human flourishing. However, many organizations lack practical methods to cultivate them. This interactive workshop introduces micro-arts practices, which involve short sessions, such as music, movement, drawing, or virtual museum visits, to promote reflection, empathy, perspective taking, and trust. Based on current research, this beginner-level, participatory session equips participants with actionable tools to integrate creative reflection into coaching, leadership, and team dynamics.

title: Setting a Cut Score Without Bleeding: Methods for Practitioners (Session ID1073)
speakers: Allie Wehling | Ashlie Plants | Kama Dodge | Morgan Tillery | Alexander Stemer
speaker_affiliations: Cisco | Merck | HackerRank | AT&T | Amazon
speaker_titles: Sr. Leader, Hiring Process & Assessment Strategy | Associate Director of Talent Assessment | Senior IO Psychologist | Senior Psychologist | Senior Research Scientist
description: Dodge, K.D., Plants, A., Stemer, A., Tillery, M.G., & Wheling, A. (2026). Setting a cut score without bleeding: Methods for practitioners [IGNITE]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Use of expert judgment about minimal competence to distinguish between passing and failing performance is a bastion in I-O psychology. In the modern testing era, factors like expert time, test design, sample size, AI availability, and other factors influence how a test cut score is set and maintained over time. This fast-paced ignite session will feature speakers from internal and external I-O psychology roles who have set cut scores using a variety of methods. Though Angoff (1971) set the gold standard for cut score methodology, this practitioner focused session will feature methodologies for solution oriented professionals.

Redefining Accountability and Leadership Identity [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel examines how AI adoption reshapes accountability in organizations, blurring responsibility once tied to leaders and teams. Panelists will explore evolving accountability structures, risks of overconfidence and shadow AI use, and shifts in leadership identity when authority is shared with algorithms. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Organizations navigating disruption collect vast employee feedback, yet traditional qualitative methods struggle to scale. This advanced tutorial shows how large language models (LLMs) can be embedded in reproducible, mixed-method pipelines to transform employee voice into actionable personas. Participants will learn to integrate HRIS and text data via ETL and RAG workflows, apply grounded theory coding and clustering, and address ethical concerns through bias auditing and oversight. Materials and code will be shared to support adoption of scalable, transparent methods.

[Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. AI shapes how people are hired, developed, and heard at work, but are these technologies amplifying inclusion or quietly rewriting inequity into systems? This panel brings together academics and practitioners to “decode” how AI affects access, belonging, and trust in organizations. Attendees will leave with insights to help organizations align technological innovation with equitable culture.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States Successful AI adoption requires not only technical skills but also behavioral and cognitive competencies. The tutorial will present the development and validation of an AI readiness framework measuring behavioral competencies (e.g. innovative mindset and openness to adopting new technologies), plus skills like proficiency in AI tools use, and cognitive abilities such as abstract reasoning. description: Lane, A., Gorbatov, S., & Crum, D. (2026). More, Better, Now: Winning with Talent. [Workshop]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Talent is the only real competitive advantage. Yet most organizations still struggle to make talent a systematic driver of performance. This interactive workshop introduces the More, Better, Now (MBN) system—a practical, research-grounded model refined over 3 decades and applied globally. Participants will learn how to get more than their fair share of high performers, upgrade the talent they already have, and move people to critical roles quickly. Authors deep dive on the MBN model and how it can be applied in practice, with the AbbVie case study illustrating MBN in action.

description: Kauth, J.M. (Co-Chair) & Forgo, E. E. (Co-Chair) (2026, April 30 - May 2). It's Not Me, It's Us: A Relational Process Perspective to Leadership and Followership Research [Symposium]. Leadership Transmission of Tight-Loose Cultural Norms Gruda, D., McCleskey, J., Al-Shammari, M., Lisak, A., Psychogios, A., Szamosi, L. T., Akowuah, L., Simha, A., Tang, M., Zaharie, M., & Tsai, C.-Y. (2026, April 30 - May 2). When Leaders Swear: Leader Profanity and Follower Dark Personality Across Economic Divides in 19 Countries Butler A.I., Kauth J.M., Forgo E.E., & Hanges P.J. (2026, April 30 - May 2) Stepping Into the Breech: Followers Reclaiming Leadership from Formal Leaders Tronetti, A.C., Byun, C., & Grand, J.A. (2026, April 30 - May 2). Eye Eye, Captain!: Exploring The Granting of Influence through Follower Eye Movement. Dickson, M.W. (Discussant) (2026, April 30 - May 2). Despite growing recognition that leadership is a reciprocal and relational process (Hanges et al., 2024; Uhl-Bien, 2014). Much research still relies on static methods that overlook its dynamic nature. This symposium features 4 studies that involve innovate methods to capture leadership as it unfolds across micro and macro contexts. Despite growing recognition that leadership is a reciprocal and relational process (Hanges et al., 2024; Uhl-Bien, 2014). This symposium features 4 studies that involve innovate methods to capture leadership as it unfolds across micro and macro contexts. These papers move leadership beyond traditional hierarchical models to illuminate how leaders and followers cocreate influence within relational, cultural, and contextual systems that broaden understanding of leadership emergence and impact.

Beyond utility: Advancing ROI in talent assessment practices [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel will explore novel approaches and business considerations for demonstrating the ROI of talent assessment practices. Traditional predictive models have provided foundational insights, and descriptive statistics are a mainstay because of simplicity and intuitiveness, but these models are often limited given the nature and pressures of business realities. A panel of applied experts will discuss topics such as data and analytic considerations, business metrics, generating stakeholder buy-in, and better aligning ROI analyses with strategic priorities and outcomes.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Meta-analysis is vital for cumulative science but remains slow and labor intensive. AI promises scale, insight, and predictive power—but also raises critical questions surrounding the risk of bias, opacity, and erosion of the profession. The session’s goal is to provide participants with concrete strategies and resources for crafting methods papers that resonate with editors and the broader scholarly community.

title: AI-Driven Assessment Development: Automating Item Generation, Fairness, and Validation (Session Id 970)
speakers: Philseok Lee | Tariq Shaban

speaker_titles: Director of Partner Success
description: Lee, P., & Shaban, T.(2026, April 30 – May 2). AI-Driven Assessment Development: Automating Item Generation, Fairness Auditing, and Factor Validation [Master Tutorial]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This Master Tutorial shows how large language models (LLMs) can accelerate assessment development while maintaining psychometric rigor. Participants will learn an AI-assisted workflow that automates item generation, readability checks, and fairness auditing; interprets embedding-based pseudofactor analyses; and embeds transparency and human oversight. Using a Curiosity miniscale, live demos illustrate readability metrics, semantic clustering, and item refinement. All materials and code will be available via GitHub for replication.

title: Award: Predicting Consequential Economic & Social Outcomes: A 50-Year Longitudinal Study (Session Id 1466)
tracks: Selection/Assessment/Individual Differences | Special Session | Award Winner
speakers: Camilla Benbow | David Lubinski
speaker_affiliations: Vanderbilt University
speaker_titles: Patricia and Rodes Hart Dean of Education and Human Development; Co-Director, Study of Mathematically Precocious Youth | Cornelius Vanderbilt Professor of Psychology; Co-Director, Study of Mathematically Precocious Youth
description: Global economies are conceptual economies—advanced by the quality of ideas. SMPY’s 50-year longitudinal study of thousands of intellectually prodigious participants (identified at age 12) shows how individual differences within the top 1% (one-third of the ability range) form contrasting constellations of specific abilities, interests, and values that predict consequential and creative outcomes in business, health care, the humanities, and STEM. The higher the abilities the more likely consequential outcomes emerge; interventions in adolescence provide a boost. In uncompensated ways, intellectually prodigious populations also contribute inordinately to the social good.

title: Religious Inclusion in the Workplace: Mega Threats, Accommodations, and ERGs (Session ID 853)
speakers: Annie Kato | Helen Chung | Denise Daniels | Humaira Hatif | Josh Nagel
speaker_affiliations: Seattle Pacific University | Wheaton College | George Mason University | New York State Unified Court System

description: Kato, A. E., (Co-Chair), Chung, H. H. (Co-Chair), & Daniels, D. (Discussant). (2026, April 30 – May 2). Religious inclusion in the workplace: Mega-threats, accommodations, & ERGs [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Despite growing employee interest in expressing religious identities at work, religion remains an understudied aspect of workplace diversity. This symposium features 3 papers that contribute to the understanding religious employees’ experiences, including the impact of mega-threats to religious identity, coworker reactions to religious accommodations, and the effects of religious employee resource groups. Collectively, this research offers new insights into how organizations can support religious employees and foster an inclusive environment for a religiously diverse workforce.

Leading through social change: How big five traits predict leaders' strategic positioning and adaptability. Harms, P. D. (2026). What if we started taking personality seriously? Leadership is critical to successful organized human conduct. However, the characteristics that help one achieve leadership status do not always help one succeed as a leader. This symposium contributes to leadership literature by uncovering personality characteristics that shape leader expectations, effectiveness, firm risk taking, and strategic orientation in different contexts.

description: Martin-Raugh, M. P. (Co-Chair) & Kell, H. J. (Co-Chair) (2026, April 30 – May 2). Advancing the Science and Practice of Situational Judgment Tests with AI. [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Situational judgment tests are widely used to assess decision-making, interpersonal skills, and applied competencies, yet traditional development remains costly and resource intensive. This symposium presents 5 innovative studies highlighting how artificial intelligence can streamline test construction, supplement subject matter expertise, generate novel stimuli, and enable automated scoring. Collectively, the papers examine tradeoffs in efficiency, validity, and fairness, offering a view of the possible future of SJTs.

Moving beyond pigeonholes and taking flight: Advances in autism at work research [IGNITE]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Although organizations have launched dedicated programs to support autistic employees, there are still pigeonholes that not only constrain autistic employees’ growth but degrade their work experience and well-being. This IGNITE! session leverages novel research to confront the typecasts, stereotypes, stigmas, neurotypical norms, and barriers faced by autistic employees in organizations. Presenters illuminate opportunities for organizational change in work design, communication practices, talent acquisition, and leadership that enable autistic employees—and their organizations—to thrive.

description: Lyon, J. S., Harrison-Garcia, A., Napper, C., Faircloth, J., & Zhang, Z. (2026). Job architecture in metaphors: The model home, the yellow brick road, and more [Alternative Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. In this high-energy IGNITE session with automatically advancing slides, practitioners will pitch the metaphors they use to explain job architecture to employees and business leaders. With a diverse slate of presenters from Lightcast, LinkedIn, Netflix, APTMetrics, and LSG Sky Chefs, and a wide range of metaphors from the yellow brick road, painkillers versus vitamins, pizza slices, shopping in grocery aisles, and selling the model home, authors know attendees will walk away with some new ideas for explaining the complex but foundational work I-O psychologists do in modern organizations. After the presentations, audience will be asked to pitch the best metaphors and wrap with a discussion of key takeaways.

description: O’Shea, P. G. (Chair). (2026, April 30 – May 2). I-O meets coaching, redux: Processes, paradox, and impact. [Symposium]. Validation of a competency model with an associated behavior measure . Showcasing I-O psychologists’ expanding process- and outcome-focused contributions to coaching research and theory building, this session highlights (a) development of a coach behavioral taxonomy through AI-based analyses of critical incidents, (b) potential synergies between a coach’s domain-specific expertise and traditional coaching approaches, and (c) a large-scale study of coaching’s impact linking client-rated coach competency assessments to a variety of outcomes.

tracks: I-O Psychologist Career Development/Teaching | Special Session | Award Winner

speaker_affiliations: University of Florida
speaker_titles: Hyatt & Cici Brown Chair in Business
description: Podsakoff, P. M. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Lessons From the "Firing Line": What Four Decades of Teaching Have Taught Me. [Alternative Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Success in the classroom comes from lessons learned serving in leadership positions before the author became an academic and as a professor during the past 4 decades in the classroom. Although some of these lessons were learned the hard way through trial and error, many of them were the result of observing the effects that the author's behavior (and the behavior of others) has on students. In this session, they will discuss these lessons, provide examples of them, and explain how they have applied the principles learned from them to teaching students at all levels.

title: Righting the Ship: Reorienting Publishing Toward Solving Important Issues (Session ID 675)
speakers: Ryan Grant | Spencer Garcia | Charles Calderwood | Cort Rudolph | Fadel Matta | Lillian Eby | Steven Rogelberg
speaker_affiliations: University of North Carolina at Charlotte | Georgia Institute of Technology | Virginia Tech | Wayne State University | University of Georgia

description: Grant, R. (co-chair), Garcia, S. C. (co-chair), Calderwood, C., Eby, L., Matta, F. K., Rogelberg, S. G. & Rudolph, C. (2026). Righting the ship: Reorienting publishing toward solving important issues [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel addresses ongoing concerns about the state of theory in the field—specifically, the lack of theory aimed at solving real-world problems (Tihanyi, 2020). Authors unpack why theory building often prioritizes novelty and “theory for theory’s sake,” examine factors fueling these trends, and discuss how this limits the field’s practical value. Panelists will outline concrete steps editors and reviewers can take to shift incentives toward theories that both advance science and address pressing organizational challenges.

Telling your story: Translating & Communicating I-O Skills [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. I-O psychology professionals and students must overcome a translation barrier between the technical language of SIOP competencies (e.g., psychometrics, job analysis) and the NACE career readiness competencies (e.g., communication, leadership) often used by employers. This panel of academics and practitioners will engage in a lively discussion on how to translate I-O expertise to bridge the gap, ensuring graduates and early career folks are marketable, able to advance through generative AI screening processes, and recognized for their valuable talent.

title: SIOP Special Session: Revenge at Work: The Psychology of Getting Even (Session ID 1447)
speakers: Nathan Bowling | P. Jewel Morrison | Thomas Tripp | Xin Wei Ong | Ashlyn Kurtz
speaker_affiliations: University of Central Florida | New College of Florida | Washington State University | The University of Akron | Clemson University
speaker_titles: Professor | Assistant Professor of Psychology | Senior Associate Dean for Academic Affairs | PhD Candidate
description: Morrison, P. J. (Co-Chair), Ong, X. W. (Co-Chair), Bowling, N. A., Kurtz, A., & Tripp, T. M. (2026). Revenge at Work: The Psychology of Getting Even [Invited Alternative Presentation]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference. New Orleans, LA, United States. Revenge at work doesn’t always look like a dramatic blow-up. It is distinct from workplace aggression as it involves action to restore justice to another party (Bies & Tripp, 2005). In today’s organizations, it often hides in the shadows: quiet quitting, malicious compliance, withholding information, or subtle sabotage towards the company or coworkers (Alter, 2015; Mahand & Caldwell, 2023). This rotating roundtable session explores how employees attempt to restore justice through retaliatory behaviors—and what that reveals about the “failure of workplace systems to offer meaningful repair."
description_html:
Morrison, P. J. (Co-Chair), Ong, X. W. (Co-Chair), Bowling, N. A., Kurtz, A., & Tripp, T. M. (2026). Revenge at Work: The Psychology of Getting Even [Invited Alternative Presentation]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference. New Orleans, LA, United States.

 

Revenge at work doesn’t always look like a dramatic blow-up. It is distinct from workplace aggression as it involves action to restore justice to another party (Bies & Tripp, 2005). In today’s organizations, it often hides in the shadows: quiet quitting, malicious compliance, withholding information, or subtle sabotage towards the company or coworkers (Alter, 2015; Mahand & Caldwell, 2023). This rotating roundtable session explores how employees attempt to restore justice through retaliatory behaviors—and what that reveals about the “failure of workplace systems to offer meaningful repair."

title: The AI Scorecard 2.0: AI’s Potential Beyond Efficiency (Session ID 977)
speakers: Katherine Gibbard | Andrew Evans | Caitlynn Sendra | Gabriela Burlacu | Lindsay Horst | Dr. Neil Morelli
speaker_affiliations: SAP SuccessFactors | Upwork | Plexus Corp. | Salesforce
speaker_titles: Senior Research Scientist | Sr. Research Scientist | Senior Research Manager | Organizational Change Manager | Sr. Dir. Workforce Transformation
description: Gibbard, K. (Chair), Sendra, C., Horst, L., Morelli, N., Burlacu, G., Evans, A. (2026). The AI Scorecard 2.0: AI’s potential beyond Efficiency [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. AI adoption is accelerating across organizations, however business gains are often underwhelming and only measured as time savings. This narrow measuring of AI impact only in terms of efficiency risks overlooking critical dimensions of organizational health, employee experience, and people sustainability. In this session, applied researchers and practitioners will present practice-based insights into how AI is shaping the workplace and discuss why organizations must measure the people impacts of AI solutions to capture business gains, and I-O psychologists can lead this charge.

title: Partner Showcase: Valence - Beyond Adoption Metrics: ADI's Power User Index for Measuring AI Coaching Effectiveness
speakers: Jennifer Carpenter | Ellie Wildman
speaker_affiliations: Valence
speaker_titles: VP, Global Head of Talent; Analog Devices | Director, Strategic Accounts
description: Organizations evaluating AI coaching typically measure adoption: logins, session counts, satisfaction. But does usage alone predict impact? This session presents findings from a study of 45,000+ AI coaching sessions at Analog Devices, a global semiconductor company with 26,000 employees across 30+ languages. Researchers developed a Power User Index measuring frequency, breadth of use cases, and conversation quality. Key findings: power users were 28% more likely to move up a performance band; manager usage doubled team adoption; and women were overrepresented among power users by 68%. Jennifer Carpenter (VP Global Head of Talent, ADI) and Ellie Wildman (Valence) will share the methodology and implications.

description: “SIOP in Space!” explores how I-O psychologists are shaping NASA missions and U.S. military space launch programs. description: Kuang, D. (Chair), Clements, L., Marentette, B., Stockdale, M. (2026). Auditing Your DEI Practices for Effectiveness and Legal Compliance. [Panel Discussion]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel offers critical insights into the evolving legal landscape of Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion (DEI) programs after Executive Order 14173, which emphasizes merit and seeks to eliminate unlawful DEI practices. Panelists will share strategies for evaluating DEI programs through a multidisciplinary lens. I-Os need to understand best practices for qualitative policy reviews that align with current law, quantitative audit techniques that evaluate the implementation of written policies through workforce analytics, and guidance for cultivating a legally defensible DEI culture.

title: More Data, Less Insight? Why So Many Leaders Still Lack Self-Awareness (Session Id 536)
speakers: Ryan Rush | Cody Warren | Kyla Holcombe | Martin Lanik
speaker_affiliations: Hogan Assessment Systems | Hogan Assessments | Visa | Pinsight

description: Rush, R., Warren, C., Malter McLean, K., Lanik, M., & Holcombe, K. (2026). More data, less insight? Why so many leaders still lack self-awareness [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Despite abundant access to feedback tools and assessment data, research indicates leadership self-awareness has not meaningfully improved in 2 decades. Panelists explore personality traits and cognitive patterns that make leaders more likely to develop self-awareness, focusing on factors that can be developed through targeted interventions. Drawing from research-backed best practices and real-world experiences across diverse organizational contexts, the discussion addresses critical questions around feedback receptivity and evidence-based approaches to building leadership self-awareness.

Underrepresented students and professionals in STEM often face structural and social barriers that threaten persistence. This symposium explores how social and science identities serve as foundations for resilience and persistence across educational and workplace contexts. Through longitudinal and qualitative approaches, the 4 papers examine predictors of STEM success, science identity development, and the lived experiences of those who persist despite adversity.

title: Coaching as a Tool for Thriving: Unlocking Growth, Resilience, and Well-Being at Work (Session ID 313)
speakers: Allie Gabriel | Brodie Riordan | Ali O'Malley, PhD, PCC | Darlene Thompson | Shonna Waters
speaker_affiliations: Purdue University, Mitch Daniels School of Business | Ocular, Georgetown University | Reflexion Group LLC | RiseOn Talent | Fractional Insights
speaker_titles: Thomas J. Howatt Chair in Management | Founder, part time faculty | Founder and Owner | Founder | CEO & Cofounder
description: Gabriel, A. S. (co-chair and panelist), Riordan, B. G. (co-chair and panelist), O’Malley, A. L. (facilitator), Thompson, D. J. (panelist), & Waters, S. D. (panelist). Coaching as a tool for thriving: Unlocking growth, resilience, and well-being at work. Session submitted to the 2026 SIOP Conference, New Orleans, LA. Employees today must adapt to extraordinary volatility, often pushed to their cognitive, emotional, and physical limits. Surviving in a chronically hypervigilant, activated, or exhausted state is unsustainable for both people and organizations. One high-impact tool for shifting from surviving to thriving is coaching. In this session, academics and practitioners who have studied and practiced coaching and thriving for combined 70+ years convene. This session will illuminate the impact that coaching has for individuals and organizations for promoting thriving, well-being, and also performance.

From Awareness to Action: Tackling Toxic Leadership Through Research and Practice [IGNITE]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Toxic leadership remains a pervasive and costly issue in today’s workplaces. This IGNITE! Panel of thought leaders and researchers highlights current research and practical strategies for detecting, measuring, and mitigating toxic leadership. Presenters will share findings from North American studies revealing both overt and covert toxic behaviors and demonstrating how evidence-based frameworks and interventions can bridge the gap between research and practice to create healthier, more accountable organizations.

Effective and Ineffective Sources of Support for Employees with Disabilities. [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Employees with disabilities continue to face known and hidden barriers to success in employment. However, limited empirical guidance is available to confidently select support strategies for these employees. This panel will highlight insights into the effective and ineffective sources of support for employees with disabilities. The panel will draw on literature across disciplines and practitioner experiences to identify science–practice gaps and provide direction for priority research endeavors and updates to workplace practices to effectively support employees with disabilities.

Predicting Ethical Leadership Behavior: Linking Simulation Outcomes with Personality and 360 Data. Ethics unfolds across multiple layers of organizations. This 50-minute symposium brings together 3 papers that illustrate these layers, offering novel insights into how ethics is shaped across various organizational levels. The first synthesizes experiments on how corporate social responsibility shapes employees’ pride, identification, and values; the second examines how ethical role models and moral intensity influence team decisions; and the third investigates leaders’ individual responses to ethical challenges. Together, they highlight practical ways to manage ethics across organizational levels.

title: The AI Conversation We Should Be Having: Real-World Harms Versus Existential Doomer Fears (Session ID 255)
tracks: Technology/Artificial Intelligence | Content Area Theme Track
speakers: Sheri Feinzig | Alan Tomassetti | David Schillinger | Julian Allen | Jane Wu
speaker_affiliations: Marsh McLennan | CPS HR Consulting | The Pennsylvania State University | Alnylam Pharmaceuticals | IBM

description: Feinzig, S., Allen, J., Schillinger, D., Tomassetti, A. & Wu, J. (2026, April 30-May 2). The AI Conversation We Should Be Having: Real-World Harms vs. Existential Doomsday Fears. Panel Discussion. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. GenAI conversations have predominantly centered around usage, adoption, and its inevitability in work and personal lives. Substantially less interest is seen in shortcomings and harms. I-O psychologists need to be aware of the negative impacts on workers and societies, and to actively seek to mitigate harm. This interactive session will engage the audience in discussions of real-world consequences of tools such as GenAI, and the role I-O psychologists should play when encountering these tools in the workplace and personally choosing to use them.

description: Black, K.J., Kath, L.M., Clark, M.A., & Baran, B.E. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Say NO to overwork and YES to I-O career sustainability! An overdue discussion [Alternative Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. I-O psychologists improve the work life of countless others, but how are they doing in their own lives? Most have experienced pressures to work excessively or impression-manage how busy and stressed they are to keep up with the crowd. This session is about self-reflection and personal resistance to norms of overwork and stress as a badge of honor. Four experts with a passion for confronting overwork culture in their research and practice will facilitate small group discussion about how to say "no" to overwork and "yes" to sustainable careers for themselves and the workers they care about.

title: Skills: Are They All We Need? (Session Id 251)
speakers: Matt Kleinman, Ph.D. | Amanda Shull Tomkoria | Brett Guidry | Ginevra Drinka | Jenna Filipkowski
speaker_affiliations: Early Warning Services | Nike | Novartis | Victoria’s Secret & Company | Federal Reserve Bank of New York
speaker_titles: VP, Head of Talent | Global VP, Talent Acquisition & Management | Director, Talent Management for Research and Development | Head of Assessment Practice
description: Kleinman, M. S., Tomkoria, A. S., Drinka, G. O., Guidry, B. W., & Filipkowski, J. N. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Skills: Are they all we need? [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Skills-based approaches to talent management have taken hold in the past several years, with companies reorienting how they hire, develop, and place key talent. However, for decades, I-Os have viewed skills as one part of a complement of capabilities required for successful performance alongside requisite knowledge, abilities, and behaviors. I-O expert practitioners will discuss whether skills are sufficient for driving high impact talent management in organizations or if traditional KSAO view needs to be reasserted in organizations to maintain core talent practice integrity.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This intermediate/advanced session addresses unprecedented organizational transformation—companies now implement 9+ major changes annually versus 2 pre-2020—whereas traditional approaches yield disappointing results. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference. New Orleans, LA, United States. Employee burnout has long been a focus of organizational research (Maslach et al., 2001). Well-being programs have been proposed to combat burnout, but employees now report fatigue from both work and wellness (Hart et al., 2018; Beames et al., 2023). Indeed, Gallup shows engagement at a 10-year low despite interventions (Harter, 2025). This session explores well-being fatigue (i.e., the distrust in wellness efforts that overlook workload, equity, and leadership) and introduces terms like carewashing and quiet cracking. Attendees will learn strategies to restore trust and tackle root causes.

title: Partner Showcase: CodeSignal - Amplifying I-O with AI: Scaling Simulation Best Practices
speakers: Seterra Riggs | Nathan Hundley
speaker_affiliations: CodeSignal
speaker_titles: Senior Talent Scientist | Lead Talent Scientist
description: Work simulations are among the strongest predictors of job performance, yet for non-technical roles they have historically been difficult to scale. Unlike coding simulations, which can be delivered and scored automatically, non-technical simulations often rely on assessment center methods such as roleplays, behavioral interviews, written exercises, and human raters. These approaches are job-relevant and effective, but also high-touch, labor-intensive, and typically reserved for executive or other high-value hiring contexts. This presentation explores how AI can serve as a force multiplier for industrial-organizational psychologists, scaling assessment best-practices without abandoning rigor that underpins them. Working in lockstep with I-O expertise, AI agents can be trained as role players, structured interviewers, and rubric-based graders using the same principles I-O practitioners have long applied to human assessors. We will discuss how AI, guided by I-O science, can support realistic, standardized, and scalable simulations for non-technical roles, and share emerging research from CodeSignal’s AI simulation products.

Exploring ethics, equity, and trust in the age of cybervetting and employee data use [Community of Interest]. Society of Industrial and Organizational Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. As organizations expand their use of digital information, from cybervetting job candidates’ social media to monitoring employees through people analytics, questions of fairness, ethics, and equity are increasingly urgent. This Community of Interest will explore the boundaries of digital data use, focusing on individual rights, organizational responsibilities, and best practices. Participants will discuss where transparency ends and intrusion begins, how to minimize bias and misuse, and what policies and practices can sustain organizational effectiveness and trust.

Diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) initiatives have recently incurred outsized backlash, with some cautioning that such initiatives may result in unintended consequences both for dominant groups who may feel excluded by them and for minoritized groups to whom they may send unwanted signals. This symposium includes 4 papers that seek to determine if, when, and for whom DEI initiatives backfire and the potential downstream implications. The goal is to better inform organizations about the potential costs and benefits of DEI initiatives.

Teaming Over Time: The Temporal Dynamics of Trust, Coordination, and Performance in Human–AI Teams (HATs) Brunell, M., & Griffin, D. J (2026). The Two-Way Street, Do AI Trust Us? Across industries, organizations are beginning to advocate for integrating AI as not just tools but active teammates. This symposium extends core team science concepts to advance understanding of these novel collaborations and how they differ from traditional human teams. Papers examine concepts across team inputs, processes, and outputs: human and AI characteristics, trust, coordination, and performance. Collectively, papers highlight both the promise and limits of applying traditional team models to human–agent teams.

Good Enough to Be Good: Talent Decisions Under Speed and Scrutiny [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Work is changing faster than most decision cycles, forcing leaders to act quickly while maintaining fairness and ROI. This panel examines how people leaders are rethinking data use, skill priorities, and motivation while adapting hiring, mobility, and development amid AI, evolving skill requirements, and economic volatility. By sharing candid lessons and linking them to concepts like unlearning and bounded rationality, panelists will show how to question outdated tools, avoid missteps, and make evidence-based, defensible talent decisions under real-world constraints.

description: Sinar, E. (Chair), Antonik. C., Fink, A., Krauss, A., & Waters, S. (2026, April 30 – May 2). GenAI and the death (or rebirth) of management. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA., United States. Authors prognosticate how the impetus of generative AI will first threaten, then dismantle, and ultimately remake the manager’s role. They discuss implications of forces such as multimodal AI, agents, and context engineering on human-AI teaming, performance evaluation, and a new form of systems thinking overseen by a reconstituted manager profile. They present previously unshared (even to each other!) predictions for GenAI's impact on management over 3, 5, 10, and 25-year time horizons and debate wildly contrasting views stated with unflinching, unwarranted confidence.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools are quickly expanding in workplace and educational settings, including their application in assessment. Although AI can enable efficiencies, scalability, and innovative approaches, users encounter challenges due to regulations and laws, the need for human oversight and governance, and complexity in evaluating the effectiveness and risks associated with AI tools. description: Kraimer, M.L., Obenauer, W.G., & Seibert, S.E. (2026, April 30-May 2). Designing and conducting constructive replication studies. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. The purpose of this introductory tutorial is to highlight the important role that replication studies play in advancing I-O psychology research and offer advice on the best practices for choosing studies to replicate, designing replication studies, and publishing them. Replication studies are those that test the same hypotheses in a previously published article largely following the same methods, but with new data and may include a few constructive modifications. The tutorial will provide participants with a “checklist” on best practices for designing and publishing replication studies.

description: Moustakis, Krystina. Mayers, Dave. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Prompt, Don’t Panic: GenAI for Essential I-O Workflows. [Alternative Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Amazon's 2015 hiring tool failed because I-Os weren't in the room. This 80-minute session tries to avoid history repeating with Generative AI. With live demonstrations, facilitators show how LLMs generate Python code for reliability analysis, factor analysis, adverse impact analysis, and predictive validation. Participants see what AI does well, where it fails, and where professional judgment remains essential. This expert demonstration is designed for AI-curious practitioners to understand how conversational prompting can accelerate psychometric workflows without sacrificing rigor or ethics.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Agentic AI moves beyond single LLMs to goal-directed systems that plan, use tools, and act with bounded autonomy. Despite their popularity, guidance on key design and implementation decisions is scarce. Artificial intelligence (AI) is rapidly transforming the landscape of training and development, offering new opportunities for efficiency, scalability, and customization. Together, these initiatives illustrate the broad potential of AI to enhance traditional training paradigms while addressing new operational demands.

title: Is ROI Dead? Debating the Value of Its Impact in Outcome Measurement (Session ID 1165)
speakers: Andrew Thurston | Bryn Chighizola | Kalifa Oliver | Mounica Reddy | Wayne Cascio | Lora Bishop | Alyssa Green
speaker_affiliations: U.S. Department of Defense | Korn Ferry | Lowe’s Home Improvement | PepsiCo | Lotis Blue Consulting
speaker_titles: Principal Research Scientist | Director, Organizational Research, Korn Ferry Institute | Sr. Director Technology | Sr. Analyst, Global Talent Management | Research Manager | Consulting Manager
description: Bishop, L. (Moderator), Cascio, W. (Presenter), Chighizola, B. (Presenter), Green, A. (Presenter), Oliver, K. (Presenter), Reddy, M. (Presenter), Thurston, A.J. (Presenter), & Adler, S. (Non-Speaking Contributor). (2026, April). Is ROI Dead? Debating the Value of Economic Impact in Outcome Measurement [Debate]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Return on investment (ROI) has long been the business standard for judging the impact of leadership and talent initiatives. Critics argue ROI oversimplifies complex outcomes—such as trust, equity, and team effectiveness—that organizations increasingly prioritize. Supporters contend ROI remains the only universal denominator, because every outcome ultimately shapes costs and benefits. This debate will stage both perspectives, invite audience challenges, and explore whether the future of measurement lies in refining ROI or moving beyond it.

Return on investment (ROI) has long been the business standard for judging the impact of leadership and talent initiatives. Critics argue ROI oversimplifies complex outcomes—such as trust, equity, and team effectiveness—that organizations increasingly prioritize. Supporters contend ROI remains the only universal denominator, because every outcome ultimately shapes costs and benefits. This debate will stage both perspectives, invite audience challenges, and explore whether the future of measurement lies in refining ROI or moving beyond it.

title: Partner Showcase: Valence - From Chatbot to Coach: Designing Systems That Actually Develop People
speakers: Elizabeth Ritterbush | Beth Shipman | Julia Walsh | Ellie Wildman | Rob Lewis
speaker_affiliations: The Home Depot | Corning Incorporated | General Mills | Valence | ADP
speaker_titles: Manager, Talent and Performance Management | Director, Culture & Talent Insights | Global Talent Assessments | Director, Strategic Accounts | Vice President, Talent Management & Org Effectiveness
description: The rapid proliferation of large language models has created a misleading assumption: that any AI system capable of conversation is capable of coaching. But I-O psychologists know that effective coaching requires context on organizational culture, individual goals, team dynamics, performance frameworks, and continuity across interactions. This panel brings together I-O leaders from Fortune 500 companies who are actively deploying AI coaching in their organizations. Panelists will explore what distinguishes a purpose-built AI coaching system from general-purpose conversational AI and the organizational design implications of scalable coaching. Whether your organization is evaluating AI coaching vendors or building internal capabilities, this session offers a practitioner-grounded framework for asking the right questions.

title: Personality and Leadership Emergence in Undergraduate Student Leaders (Session ID 119)
speakers: Chase Winterberg | Daniel King | Anne Greenhalgh
speaker_affiliations: Hogan Assessments | Talent Strategies Consulting | The Wharton School, University of Pennsylvania
speaker_titles: Director of the Hogan Research Institute | Principal Consultant | Senior Director, McNulty Leadership Program & Adjunct Professor of Management
description: Greenhalgh, A. (Panelist), King, D. (Panelist), and Winterberg, C. (Panelist). (2026, April 30 – May 2). Personality and Leadership Emergence in Undergraduate Student Leaders [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This presentation examines how personality relates to early leadership emergence among undergraduate teaching assistants (TAs) in a gateway leadership development course for undergraduate incoming students. Sample includes 5,967 undergraduates, including 132 TAs, and a comparison group of early-career professionals under 30. Authors argue that personality assessments, when used formatively, can guide leadership development within academic settings. This introductory session is intended for educators and practitioners seeking to identify and support emerging leaders in higher education.

title: How to Leverage Employee Listening Data to Effectively Drive Change (Session ID 1280)
speakers: Erica Pachmann | Linden Wooderson | Rhonda Buseman
speaker_affiliations: Culture Amp | The Guitar Center Company | Benchmark Electronics · Full-time
speaker_titles: Lead People Scientist | Sr Manager, People Systems & Analytics | SVP and Chief Human Resources Officer
description: Buseman, R., Pachmann, E., Sa, M., Voyles, E., & Wooderson, L. (2026, April). How to leverage employee listening data to effectively drive change [Conference proposal, Alternative (hybrid): Case studies & interactive Q&A]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference. It’s no secret change is difficult to implement, especially within large organizations. In fact, research studies have estimated that roughly 60%–70% of change initiatives fail. One of the keys to setting up an effective change management strategy is starting with employee listening. From there, the session will include case studies from 3 organizations where presenters will share their experiences and recommendations for the implementation of effective change. Attendees will gain concrete actionable ideas to drive meaningful change within their organizations.

title: Stick the Landing: Reducing Early Risk of New Leader Failure (Session ID 227)
speakers: Jessie McClure | Lauren Wallace | Mina Morris | Robin Cohen
speaker_affiliations: Hogan Assessments | Google | Accenture | Robin Cohen Leadership
speaker_titles: Senior Consultant | Researcher, Performance Management Design | Head of Assessments
description: McClure, J., Lazarus, E., Cohen, R.., Morris, M., & Wallace, L. (2026). Stick the landing: Reducing early risk of new leader failure [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel explores the current evidence-based practices for reducing the high failure rate of leaders within their first 18 months. Because leader failure follows predictable patterns, panelists will use a chronological framework to examine interventions to transform potential adversarial relationships to productive partnerships. Drawing from industrial-organizational psychology research and real-world implementation experiences, the discussion addresses critical factors that predict new leader success with particular emphasis on change agent leaders who face heightened scrutiny and resistance.

title: Beyond Design: When I-O Psychologists Cross the Assessment Divide (Session ID 442)
speakers: Mike Wojcik | Kelsey Byerly | Menucha Sperlin | Nick Walion | Pamela Baumer | Robert Minjock
speaker_affiliations: Human Resources Research Organization | PDRI by Pearson | Port Authority of New York New Jersey | HumRRO | Fairfax County Government

description: Wojcik, M. F. (Chair), Baumer, P.C., Byerly, K.R., Minjock, R.M., Sperlin, M., Walion, N.J. (2026). Beyond design: When I-O psychologists cross the assessment divide [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Although I-O psychologists typically develop tests that others administer, many practitioners, especially those who partner with public safety agencies, wear both hats by administering the assessments they design. Understanding test administration realities helps assessment developers maximize the effectiveness of their tests. Panelists with experience developing and administering assessments will share strategies for maintaining objectivity, ensuring standardization, and responding resiliently to unexpected events while preserving test integrity—lessons applicable across all assessment contexts.

title: Ways to Address Flawed Assumptions in Testing (Session ID 452)
speakers: Dr. Joel P. Wiesen
speaker_affiliations: Applied Personnel Research
speaker_titles: Director
description: Wiesen, J. P. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Ways to Address Flawed Assumptions in Testing [Tutorial]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Testing practices often rely on unnoticed flawed assumptions or misconceptions. For example, high interrater reliability in BARS grading may be artifactual and not support validity, and setting passing points based on overall test performance assumes that strengths compensate for weaknesses and may result in hiring incompetent applicants. Possible remedies for 20 such flaws will be discussed from the viewpoint of a practitioner.

title: The Dark Side of Meaningful Work: Passion, Burnout, and Organizational Responsibility (Session ID 1300)
speakers: Malissa Clark | Javon Davis | Shelby Davis
speaker_affiliations: University of Georgia | Colorado State University
speaker_titles: Graduate Research & Teaching Assistant
description: Davis, S. N. (Co-Chair), Clark, M. (Co-Chair), Davis, J. (2026). The Dark Side of Meaningful Work: Meaning, Burnout, and Organizational Responsibility [ALTERNATIVE SESSION]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA., United States. Although meaningful work is often positioned as the facilitator of employee well-being and engagement, emerging research highlights troubling consequences: overwork, burnout, and tolerance of poor working conditions. This session challenges previous meaningful work literature by engaging the audience in a discussion on the potential consequences of meaningful work perceptions. Through an Open Fishbowl structure, attendees will connect real-world examples with research on meanings work, callings, and occupational health.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. High-potential (HiPo) programs can be powerful tools for developing future talent, yet even small design missteps can limit their impact and create unintended consequences such as wasted resources, frustration, or turnover. Attendees will gain actionable insights to strengthen their programs and maximize organizational impact.

speaker_affiliations: CodeSignal

description: Hundley, N. A. & Riggs, S. (2026, April 30 - May 2). Beyond correlation: A practical guide to causal analytics for I-O psychologists [Master Tutorial]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This introductory tutorial equips participants with practical tools to apply causal inference methods to everyday workplace challenges. Through accessible explanations and example scenarios, authors introduce 3 quasi-experimental analytical approaches not commonly taught in the field of I-O psychology: differences-in-differences, regression discontinuity design, and propensity score matching. Participants will learn when and how to apply each method, as well as the critical assumptions and potential pitfalls associated with each.

description: Schnure, K. (Chair), November, P., Schiro, T., & Williamson, J. (2026, April). The C-suite partnership that drives performance: A CEO and CHRO in conversation. Fireside chat presented at the annual conference of the Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology, New Orleans, LA. This fireside chat brings together Ochsner Health CEO Pete November and Chief People and Culture Officer Tracey Schiro for an intimate conversation about the partnership dynamics that drive organizational performance. The discussion moves beyond typical panel presentations to explore how these leaders actually work together daily. Through candid dialogue and real stories from Louisiana's largest health system, they examine partnership models, trust-building in the C-suite, data-driven decision making, and more, offering practical insights grounded in both research and lived experience.

description: Drake, M. R. (Co-Chair), Burns, G. N. (Co-Chair). (2026, April 30 – May 2). Insufficient effort responding in survey research: Impact, mitigation, and management [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Burns, G. N., Chen, X., McFerran, M. W., & Mochinushi, Y. (2026). Impact of Content Nonresponsivity on Model Fit and Network Features Across Cultures. Drake, M. R., Baldwin, N., Sizemore, S., Wilson, R. & Ramsey, M. C. (2026). Reducing Insufficient Effort Responding with Instructional Manipulations. McFerran, M. W., Mochinushi, Y., & Burns, G. N. (2026). Careless responding in surveys: Multilevel insights from interviewer evaluations. Gibson, A. M., Roebke, M. A., & Bowling, N. A. (2026). Measuring Careless Responding with Infrequency Scales: Five Coding Approaches. Harris, K. N., Drake, M. R., Mouton, A. N., Deregla, A. & Ramsey, M. C. (2026). Beyond Crowdsourcing Platforms: The Importance of IER Detection Across Sample Types. This symposium presents recent advances in research regarding the measurement of careless responding and effects careless responding on data quality. Specifically, this proposal includes 5 papers that not only extend previous research, but explore unique, previously unstudied interventions in one of the following areas: (a) impact of not accounting for IER, (b) antecedents and mitigation strategies to decrease IER rates, and (c) management of IER, including sample considerations and detection methods.

From Graduate School to Practice: Navigating the Early Years as an I-O Professional [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel aims to examine the career transition from graduate school to the workplace, focusing on the critical period between securing a role in the field and establishing one’s professional identity. Authors bring together a group of panelists who will discuss their experiences in building relationships, establishing credibility, and overcoming challenges unique to I-O psychologists. The aim of this session is to help early-career I-O practitioners transition from graduate training to their careers by equipping attendees with strategies for navigating workplace dynamics and self-advocacy.

The Government Relations and Advocacy Committee (GREAT) in collaboration with SIOP’s Presidential Executive Trio and Lewis-Burke Associates – SIOP’s contracted federal advocacy firm – will give an overview on and discussion about the current state of Federal Affairs and scientific advocacy relevant to SIOP’s interests. title: Automated Anonymous: Education and Reskilling for I-Os in the Age of Automation (Session ID 964)
speakers: Steven Toaddy | Shawn Bergman | Eric Kuo
speaker_affiliations: Appalachian State University | Uplevel
speaker_titles: Kulynych/Cline Distinguished Professor of Psychology | Organizational Research Scientist
description: Bergman, S. M., Kuo, E. W., & Toaddy, S. R. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Automated anonymous: Education and reskilling for I-Os in the age of automation [Panel discussion]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Automation of some of the tasks that are currently the bread and butter of I-O careers will necessitate changes both in how I-Os train the next generation and how they retrain themselves. This panel unites academic and applied perspectives to explore, first, what I-O activities are foreseen to be automated in the coming years and, second, how each of formal graduate programs and personal reskilling can equip I-Os to thrive alongside (or when supplanted by) intelligent systems, promoting our personal and professional flourishing in an increasingly automated world.

Building the plane as we fly it: Utilizing interns to pilot new programs [Alternative Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Internships offer valuable opportunities for departments and projects to benefit from fresh perspectives and emerging talent. Authors feature a panel of I-O psychologists at various career stages, and panelists will share personal insights from both sides of the internship. Attendees will learn the long-term benefits of developing intern talent and how they can serve as a pipeline for future full-time employees. This session aims to inspire organizations to view internships as strategic tools for building stronger, more agile teams.

description: Hyland, P. K., Bruck, C., Caputo, A., Reeves, D. (2026). Assessing the State of the Field: A Reflexive Community Conversation [Alternative Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. In recent years, various I-O psychologists have raised questions and concerns about the state of the field, with some arguing that I-Os are experiencing a collective identity crisis. In this 50-minute alternative session, 4 members of the I-O community invite attendees to engage in a highly participative conversation about the state of the field. Using a world café format, attendees will have the opportunity to explore a series of reflexive questions via collaborative, facilitated discussions with their peers.

description: Watts, L. (Chair), Connell, J. B., Weiner, J.A., & Guentert, K. (2026). Ethical AI in I-O Practice: From Member Insights to Practical Considerations [Alternative Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. The Committee for the Advancement of Professional Ethics (CAPE) surveyed SIOP members in June 2025 on ethical AI use in I-O work. Members of the committee will provide the results of the survey and introduce a reference document which is a practical guide on ways to use AI in practice, and which links to topics raised in the survey. This session will describe the survey results and explore ethical guidelines, and educational resources. Authors will discuss ethical frameworks, operational considerations, and practical questions regarding AI use.

speakers: Marlee Moore | Brent Oja | Matt Huml | Don Zhang

description: Moore, M. J. (Chair), Huml, M. (Panelist), Mathieu, J. (Panelist), Oja, B. (Panelist), & Zhang, D. (Panelist). (2026). Huddling Up to Level Up: Advancing I-O Psychology through Sport (& Vice Versa) [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Interdisciplinary research conducted at the intersection of I-O psychology and sport has the potential to advance both fields in meaningful ways; however, communication between the 2 fields remains limited. This session brings together expert researchers in I-O psychology and sport management to discuss the opportunities, challenges, and future research integrating the 2 fields. The goal of this session is to connect those with common interest in sport while providing guidance and inspiration for future work that advances the field of I-O psychology through sport.

title: Community of Interest: Making Invisible Labor Visible: Hidden Work and Org. Success (Session ID 1448)
tracks: Motivation, Attitudes, and Emotions | Special Session

speaker_affiliations: Research Assistant | University of Texas at Arlington

description: Pena, D., Gallegos, E., Kosak-Koberna, K., Zhu, S., (2026, April 29-May 2). Making Invisible Labor Visible: How Hidden Contributions Shape Organizational Success [Community of Interest]. Society of Industrial and Organizational Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Invisible labor—tasks like mentoring, onboarding, and emotional support—sustains workplace performance yet often falls disproportionately on certain employees. This Community of Interest will explore why valuing and recognizing these contributions matters for organizational effectiveness and culture. Discussions will focus on change fatigue, emotional labor and exhaustion, leadership’s role, equitable recognition, and fairness in distributing career implications.

speakers: Kaytlynn Griswold | Michelle (Mikki) Hebl | Kisha Jones | Michael Hosie | Sertrice Shipley

Demystifying inclusion: Clearing the fog around inclusive actions & behaviors [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Inclusion is widely valued but often misunderstood. Many employees want to contribute to inclusive workplaces yet lack clear, practical guidance. This panel, Demystifying Inclusion, brings together practitioners, scholars, and consultants to clarify what inclusion truly means, strip away its politicized framing, and highlight actionable behaviors that foster respect, fairness, and belonging. Through research and real-world examples, panelists will illuminate simple, scalable paths to inclusion.

title: Is Laughter the Best Medicine? How Should (Or Shouldn't) I-Os Prescribe Humor at Work (Session ID 429)
speakers: Brittany Head | Amy Grubb | Bradley Wilson | Kyle Page | Tyler Kempf | William Macey
speaker_affiliations: Perceptyx | Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) | Perceptyx, Inc. | Nestlé USA | The Second City

description: Head, B., Macey, W., Wilson, B., Kempf, T., Grubb, A., Granger, B., Pelosi, E. (2026). “Is Laughter the Best Medicine? How Should (or Shouldn't) IO's Prescribe Humor at Work. [Panel Discussion] Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This interactive panel, featuring experts from Apple, the FBI, The Second City, and other highly regarded organizations, explores how humor strengthens inclusive, engaged, and innovative work cultures. Authors examine how tenets of humor—such as active listening, building on others’ ideas, and embracing uncertainty—enhance resilience, collaboration, creativity and psychological safety in diverse teams. Panelists highlight studies linking humor with employee engagement and belonging among both neurodivergent and neurotypical employees. Attendees gain evidence-based insights and practical approaches.

This interactive panel, featuring experts from Apple, the FBI, The Second City, and other highly regarded organizations, explores how humor strengthens inclusive, engaged, and innovative work cultures. Authors examine how tenets of humor—such as active listening, building on others’ ideas, and embracing uncertainty—enhance resilience, collaboration, creativity and psychological safety in diverse teams. Panelists highlight studies linking humor with employee engagement and belonging among both neurodivergent and neurotypical employees. Attendees gain evidence-based insights and practical approaches.

title: Community of Interest: AI Gone Wrong—Lessons From the Trenches (Session ID 1453)
speakers: Cassie Colton | Patrick Rolwes | Dr. Jacob Seybert

description: Seybert, J., Colton, C., & Rolwes, P., ( 2026). Community of Interest: AI Gone Wrong: Lessons From the Trenches. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Much of the conversation around AI in organizations emphasizes its promise and potential, but real-world implementation often reveals serious pitfalls. From hallucinations and bias blind spots to privacy concerns and overreliance on tools, failures can undermine trust, fairness, and outcomes. This Community of Interest session invites participants to candidly share “AI gone wrong” experiences and surface lessons learned. Through discussion, participants will gain insights into recurring challenges and sharpen their awareness of how to navigate AI’s risks responsibly.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Many I-O psychologists have jobs that require communicating complex information to diverse audiences including executives, clients, and stakeholders, but formal training in technical writing may not be part of their academic preparation. Society of Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Though highly skilled, I-O psychologists often struggle to influence decision-makers. This session teaches Monroe's Motivated Sequence (MMS), a framework used since the 1930s to craft persuasive messages. After last year’s successful session (350+ RSVPs and 100 attendees) this year’s will focus where participants were most engaged: panelist stories and live pitch critiquing. Presenters will share real scenarios where they successfully influenced stakeholders. Then they will crowdsource pitch ideas from the audience using the framework.

[Contributor] (2026). Detecting and dealing with GenAI-facilitated cheating [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. With the rapid advances in Generative AI (GenAI) capabilities over the last few years, job applicants are using widely available tools like ChatGPT to write resumes and cover letters, and to prepare for interviews. Some also attempt to outsource the assessment completion process to GenAI. This session brings together global perspectives on detecting the use of GenAI to cheat on assessments, as well as dealing with the consequences. Panelists from several multinational organizations will engage in a lively discussion on how to detect and cope with GenAI-facilitated cheating.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. I-O psychology distinguishes between “in-role” and “extra-role” behaviors, yet the line between them is often unclear. This interactive session uses film clips from The Devil Wears Prada, Horrible Bosses, and The Intern to explore that ambiguity. Participants will vote on whether actions are in-role or extra-role as scholars discuss how context, expectations, and measurement shape perceptions of what it truly means to go “above and beyond” at work.

Graduate students often experience various forms of remoteness during their training (e.g., distance from advisor, lab, or program). Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Coachability—the willingness to accept feedback, reflect, and adapt—is a key predictor of leadership success, yet it is inconsistently measured across assessment methods. This session brings together researchers and practitioners to explore how coachability appears in simulations, psychometrics, and interviews, and what signals reveal a leader’s learning capacity. Panelists will share evidence and practical strategies to assess and develop coachability as a foundation for leadership adaptability and growth.

title: Beyond High Touch: AI in Executive Development (Session ID 302)
speakers: Gerard Dulac | Lilly Lin | Lamar Pierce | Kyle Sandell | Sergey Gorbatov

description: Dulac, G. R. (Chair), Gorbatov, S., Lin, L., Pierce, L., & Sandell, K. J. (2026). Beyond high touch: AI in executive development [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Artificial Intelligence (AI) is transforming learning and development at every level of organizations—personalizing learning, scaling leadership programs, and enhancing coaching. Yet, paradoxically, executive development remains largely untouched, still centered on high-cost, high-touch human interventions. This panel explores whether “high touch” must always mean “human touch,” and how AI can democratize, scale, and reimagine executive learning. Panelists will discuss key use cases and debate the opportunities and risks of applying AI at the top of organizations.

title: Wait What!? Disparate Impact Theory and the Current Legal and Regulatory Landscape (Session ID 1278)
speakers: Charles Mcghee | Patrick McNiel | David Morris
speaker_affiliations: Affirmity | Workforce Compliance & Diversity | Affirmity
speaker_titles: Manager and Statistician | Principal Consultant
description: McGhee, C. R., McNiel, P. D., Morris, M. D. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Wait What!? - Disparate Impact Theory and the Current Legal and Regulatory Landscape [IGNITE]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Disparate impact theory has been embedded in the legal and regulatory landscape for over 50 years. This legal doctrine has been upheld by court rulings at all levels and has broadened in application over the years. However, disparate impact theory as a guiding civil rights principal is being threatened by recent Supreme Court rulings, executive orders, employer confusion about “illegal DEI,” and other forms of pressure. Panelists will present the history of this theory, recent changes in the legal and regulatory landscape, effects on employers, and outcomes that are evolving as a result.

LLM-based Scoring in Text-based Fit Assessments. Large language models are transforming psychological measurements. This symposium features 4 studies exploring LLMs as collaborators in key measurement tasks. The first evaluates LLMs for social desirability ratings in forced-choice item design. The second compares LLM- and human-based content validation across more than 100 organizational scales, offering interpretive benchmarks. The third introduces AutoScale, a fully automated LLM pipeline for scale development with strong psychometric properties. The 4th applies LLMs to score person–organization fit from open-ended responses.
