title: Synthetic Personality: Conceptual and Research Encounters (Session ID 543)
speakers: Georgi Yankov | Anoop Javalagi | Bradley Pitcher | Pengda Wang
speaker_affiliations: Hogan Assessments | HumRRO | APTMetrics | Rice University
speaker_titles: Senior Scientist of Assessment Innovation | Research Scientist | Senior Consultant & Research Scientist
description: Yankov, G. P. (Chair) (2026). Synthetic personality: Conceptual and research encounters [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This symposium investigates whether large language models can emulate human-like personalities consistent with the 5 factor model. It showcases 4 studies examining the stability of LLM-based decision policies, the effects of personality manipulation in workplace negotiations, the validity of LLM-generated personality ratings compared to self-reports, and the fidelity of simulated profiles such as those of U.S. presidents. Findings suggest LLMs can exhibit stable, theoretically coherent, and psychometrically valid “synthetic personalities.”

description: Paustian-Underdahl, S. C. (Chair), Banks, G., Cullen-Lester, K., Frear, K., Loignon, A., & Louie, R. (Panelists). (2026, April). Bridging the divide: How leadership science can better inform leadership development [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Leadership development (LD) is a multi-billion-dollar industry, yet programs often fail to deliver sustained impact. Leadership science has identified the traits and behaviors linked to effectiveness, but these insights are often lost before or during translation into practice. At the same time, research frequently overlooks the realities leaders face. This panel examines the systemic science–practice gap in leadership: why evidence is underutilized, where research lacks relevance, and how collaboration can bridge the divide between scholarship and LD practice.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, Denver, CO, United States. Practitioners face growing challenges in generating and maintaining assessment validity evidence amid rising threats from plagiarism, AI misuse, limited performance criteria, and reduced SME availability. title: Job Insecurity in the Age of Automation: Individual, Team, and Global Perspectives (Session ID 342)
speakers: Andrea Bazzoli | Morgan Zipfel | Paisley Shultz | Hannah A. Crandell | Minseo Choi

description: Zipfel, M., & Bazzoli, A. (Co-Chairs) (2026, April 30 – May 2). Job Insecurity in the Age of Automation: Individual, Team, and Global Perspectives [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. The rapid deployment of AI, robotics, and algorithmic systems has led to technology-led workforce reductions, reshaping not only headcount, but also how individuals view their futures, how teams function under pressure, and how global narratives on technology evolve. This symposium unites individual, team, and global lenses to explore workers’ experiences of job insecurity and technological change, highlighting evidence-based, theory-informed pathways for responsible deployment, targeted reskilling, and healthier, more ethical organizations in the age of automation.

Secret agents of DEI: Incorporating best practices into organizations [Alternative Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States Amid anti-DEI backlash, organizations are reducing DEI efforts and devoting fewer resources to this area. However, there is still a need to advance this work. Authors propose that employees can be “secret agents” of DEI: individuals without formal DEI job titles who promote equity in various policies, procedures, and practices through their work. The session will feature practitioners who incorporate DEI into their daily jobs, followed by an academic discussing how scholars can conduct research to aid the work of DEI secret agents.

description: Waclawski, J. (Moderator), Adler, S., Church, A.H., Rogelberg, S.G. & Seaton, G. (2026). Rethinking talent management: Challenging conventional assumptions. [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans LA, United States In today's fast-paced business landscape, where agility is paramount, organizations must regularly reassess their traditional views on how best to manage talent. Several of the core assumptions that have long guided talent management practices may no longer hold true. This session re-examines some key assumptions that organizations often make concerning talent management, drawing from findings emerging from I-O science and insights of leading scientist/practitioners. By reconsidering these assumptions, organizations can start to cultivate alternative, more effective, ways of managing talent.

description: Nye, Christopher (Chair), Burrows, C., Yang, J., & Black, E. (2026). AI in the Workplace: Reflections and Guidance from EEO Experts [Panel Discussion]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Artificial intelligence (AI) is rapidly changing the workplace. For this session, Charlotte Burrows, former Chair of the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission (EEOC), and Jenny Yang, former Chair of the EEOC and former Director of the Office of Federal Contract Compliance Programs (OFCCP), will discuss their views of the current legal landscape surrounding AI in hiring and how I-O psychology can contribute to this discussion. Then, along with a colleague, they will discuss approaches that computer scientists use to help prevent bias in AI models and the legal implications of these methods.

The purpose of this panel discussion is to explore the utility of nudge interventions for enhancing leader development, drawing insights across practitioners and academics. Topics addressed include nudge theory and core components of nudging, current organizational applications, proposed applications toward leaders, and potential considerations and challenges in their future adoption. Theory building in organizational psychology and behavior has advanced greatly over the last several decades but still needs to better uncover the processes driving phenomena over time. We argue for the ability to triangulate process knowledge through the combined use of qualitative and quantitative process data, along with computational process theories. Each of these approaches provides unique but meaningful insights into the nature of organizational processes to build better theories and accumulate knowledge. Authors demonstrate this tripartite approach across 5 studies of teamwork processes.

Beyond Disengagement: A Multidimensional Framework for Understanding Quiet Quitting Mercado, B. K. & Dilchert, S. (2026). Cyberloafing: A Distinct Manifestation of Withdrawal Ren, Z., Ones, D. S., & Giordano, C. (2026). Emotional Intelligence and Employee Disengagement: A Meta-Analysis. When withdrawal was conceptualized, employees were co-located and physically present meant psychologically engaged. Not anymore. Authors introduce an expanded framework encompassing contemporary forms such as quiet quitting, cyberloafing, disengagement, particularly invisible in remote/hybrid work. This symposium features 4 studies exploring withdrawal as theoretically distinct from other counterproductive behaviors and highlighting its personality nomological net. Findings from the meta-analyses and primary studies in this session are essential for anyone addressing engagement and retention at work.

title: Stress as Offense to Self: New Understanding of Illegitimacy at Work (Session ID 1215)
tracks: Occupational Health/Work and Family | Content Area Theme Track
speakers: Marcus Fila | Wiston Rodriguez | DuBois Barnes | Kelly Nguyen | Marcel Kern | Taylor Flinn
speaker_affiliations: Hope College | San Diego State University | Auburn University | Baruch College | University of Göttingen
speaker_titles: Associate Professor Of Management | Assistant Professor | Ph.D. Student Researcher | Professor of Work and Organizational Psychology
description: Fila, M. J. (Co-Chair), & Rodriguez, W. A. (Co-Chair). (2026, April 30-May 2). Stress As Offense-to-Self: New Understanding of Illegitimacy at Work [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Illegitimate tasks are professional identity threatening stressors that violate normative prescriptions of what can be expected in a given role or position. By signaling a lack of respect towards employees and their roles, they leave employees feeling like “I should not have to do this!” Research on illegitimate tasks spans 17 years. However, new outcomes and boundary conditions continuing to be examined. Therefore, the purpose of this symposium is to highlight 5 novel, new findings in this research; including the first meta-analytic review of this construct, and a study of a new and related construct: the illegitimate absence of tasks.

title: Will AI Save or Replace Assessment Centers? A Debate on the Future (Session ID 393)
speakers: Brad Chambers | Christina Norris-Watts | Doug Reynolds | Lynn Collins, Ph.D. | Michael Bazigos | Suzanne Tsacoumis

description: Chambers, B.A., Collins, L.G, Bazigos, M.N., Norris-Watts, C., Reynolds, D., & Tsacoumis, S. (2026, April 30-May 2). Will AI save or replace assessment centers? A debate on the future. [Panel Discussion]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Artificial intelligence (AI) is rapidly transforming assessment centers, promising scalability, consistency, and predictive power. But can algorithms ever replace what makes ACs unique: observing live behavior, its consequences, and the powerful feedback and organizational learning that flow from it? In this debate, 4 panelists will take strong, opposing stances on motions such as “AI is more valid than human assessors” and “AI will render assessment centers obsolete.” Expect sparks to fly as authors confront validity, fairness, ethics, and the future relevance of assessment centers.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Emotions have become a central aspect of leadership in today’s evolving workplace. This symposium offers multidisciplinary perspectives on leadership as a nuanced emotional process. Four papers examine antecedents of leaders’ emotional influence, follower interpretations, relational dynamics, and the emotional costs leaders face when handling others’ emotions. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Ishola, S., Hickman, L., & Koutsoumpis, A. (2026). Paraverbal cues and personality: A review of organizational research and demonstration of modern methods. Bonner, C. V., Woo, S. E., & Waters, S. (2026). Volitional personality change for professional growth: A multi-layered investigation via a digital intervention. This symposium presents 4 contributions which each introduce novel perspectives to the science of personality at work. These innovations involve (a) the use of paraverbal cues as relevant sources of information, (b) the value of life narratives for personality assessment in organizations, (c) the analysis and validity of within-person variability in personality characteristics across assessments, and finally (d) the effectiveness of an intervention aimed at intentionally changing aspects of personality in the context of professional development.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Dickson, M. Discussant and facilitator. Organizations create and curate large quantities of research-friendly data. For more than a decade the use of this data has been predicted to revolutionize IO science. Is the revolution here? This symposium reports evidence on the impact of such data on IO science, examines 2 recent success stories in acquiring and using organizational data for published research, and closely examines the nature of academic-organization collaboration for data sharing and scientific impact. The symposium combines research findings with experience-based insights.

description: Jefferis, B., Lam, A., Williams, B., Witherspoon, C., & Wright, M. (2026, April). Taking action on survey data with a deskless workforce [Alternative session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA. The purpose of this session is to learn from practitioners who work with deskless workers to take action on survey data to improve the employee experience. Although deskless workers make up the majority of the global workforce and are often included in employee listening surveys, this group is often overlooked in the postsurvey phase of taking action. This session will provide a platform for the panelists to share realistic learnings and solutions to real-world challenges. The audience will have opportunities to help us prioritize the direction of the conversation and ask direct questions.

E., London, M., Phillips, J., Reindel, R., Zickar, M. J., & Catlin, M. (2026, April 30 – May 2). (Re)Connecting occupational health psychology with labor unions [Alternative session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. It is a little-known fact that the field of occupational health psychology was developed in part by I-O psychologists who studied and worked with labor unions. Although most of those connections have faded, interest in their reestablishment is increasing. This session will facilitate that process through roundtable discussions between session attendees, union leaders, and occupational health and safety researcher-activists. They will confront disconnects between I-O/OHP and organized labor, learn about what communities have to offer each other, and lay the groundwork for future collaboration.

(2026, April 30 – May 2). What is your dangerous idea? Reimagining the future of employee surveys [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Are I-Os too comfortable with the conventional employee survey playbook? This session brings together survey practitioners from diverse organizations to explore “dangerous ideas” that challenge long-held assumptions in employee surveying. These ideas provoke new ways of thinking and the unsettling possibility that they might be true. In this modified panel format, panelists will explore topics such as the role of surveys in the age of AI, the (over)emphasis on engagement metrics, and how scorecard incentives can distort data. This session invites attendees to reimagine the future of employee listening.

description: Ali, A. A. (Co-Chair), Panton, K. (Co-Chair), Aruffo, A. (Co-Chair), (2026, April 30 – May 2). Are You Smarter than an MBA? An AI-Powered Upskill on Business & Financial Acumen [Master Tutorial]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This interactive session will upskill I-O psychologists on key tenets of business and financial acumen through AI-powered teaching. title: Partner Showcase: Aon Talent Solutions - Work Rewired: AI's Impact on the Workforce, Talent Assessment, and the Candidate Experience
speakers: Ernie Paskey | Melissa Champine | Lauren Lilly | Maged Natanios | Scott Reithel
speaker_affiliations: Aon | Aon Talent Assessments | Marriott International
speaker_titles: Leader for Workforce Transformation | Partner, Head of North America Assessment Practice | Director, Talent Management & Research | Managing Vice President, Talent Management and Sr HR Partner | Associate Partner, People Analytics
description: The showcase examines how Artificial Intelligence (AI) is reshaping work—and how organizations can respond with intention rather than reaction. The session introduces an AI sensitivity–driven approach to understanding how tasks, skills, and roles are evolving, and how those insights enable job reimagination, targeted skill updates, and more informed workforce decisions. Building on this foundation, presenters explore how AI is transforming talent assessment and the candidate’s experience, shifting from traditional methods to more scalable, immersive, and engaging solutions. The session concludes with a real-world case study illustrating how AI can be used to design next‑generation assessment experiences, including the creation of dynamic video, audio, and 3D content—grounding emerging concepts in practical application for I-O and talent leaders.

Invisible disabilities pose unique identity challenges at work because disclosure is optional, yet these conditions can influence behavior and performance. This symposium examines how employees manage invisible disabilities and the associated costs and benefits. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Pregnancy Loss at Work: Disenfranchised and Embodied Grief Amid Organizational Norms and Policies Contrary to how research and policy focus on pregnancy and postpartum transitions, other maternal and reproductive experiences of working women remain overlooked. This symposium highlights 3 such areas: egg freezing, fertility treatment, and pregnancy loss. Across qualitative and quantitative studies, the papers examine how employees navigate disclosure, emotions, and organizational support when facing these experiences. Together, they reveal critical gaps in workplace policies and current literature, and with the discussant, the symposium seeks to integrate these contributions, pointing toward the need for more inclusive practices and a broader research agenda.

The aim of this panel is to explore the evolving phenomenon of job hopping, focusing on its motivations, perceptions, and implications for employees and organizations. Beyond engagement: Tackling organizational disconnection in modern work psychology [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Despite digital connectivity and AI tools, employees report rising loneliness and disengagement. This panel explores why engagement surveys fail to address the root cause, organizational disconnection, and examines its impact on culture, performance, and well-being. Drawing on research in work psychology, hybrid work, and AI-mediated sociality, authors propose connection as a fundamental part of organizations’ employee value proposition (EVP).

speakers: Morgan Rose Taylor | David Ostberg | Jennifer Cullen | Kristin Saboe | Stephanie Andel
speaker_affiliations: Culture Amp | Medallia | Visa | Google | Dell Technologies

description: Taylor, M.R. (Chair), Andel, S., Cullen, J., Pelosi, E., Saboe, K., & Ostberg, D. (2026, April 30 – May 2). When engagement becomes a performance metric: Ensuring fair use in evaluation [Panel Discussion]. Society for Industrial Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Organizations often include engagement scores as part of manager performance criteria to drive accountability and improve team outcomes. However, this can place undue pressure on managers, as many factors affecting engagement lie beyond their control. This raises a key question: How can organizations ensure fair use of engagement scores in evaluations? In this panel, experts in employee listening will explore diverse perspectives on the benefits and challenges of this approach and share best practices to balance fairness and accountability.

description: Bisbey, T. (Co-Chair), Masser, J. (Co-Chair), & Harris-Watson, A. M. (Co-Chair). (2026, April 30 – May 2). Beyond average: Advances in team composition and approaches to multilevel data [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Harris-Watson, A. M., Masser, J., Romero, M., & Bisbey, T. (2026, April 30 – May 2). From traits to trait preferences: The role of implicit teammate theories. Carter, D. (2026). Discussant Johnson, J., & Traylor, A. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Proactive personality and team conflict: The moderating role of constructive controversy training in academic research teams. Nguyen, Q., Nguyen, J., Rousseau, M., Grossman, R., & Feitosa, J. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Temporal and dyadic perspectives on diversity, trust, and performance. Despite decades of research on team composition, I-Os still lack consistent answers about how to assemble people into effective teams. This symposium goes beyond traditional approaches. Authors examine how personality dispersion influences conflict over time and whether training interventions help, explore temporal dynamics showing how surface and deep-level diversity operate differently than previously thought, and introduce teammate expectations as a missing link between traits and outcomes. These studies offer fresh perspectives on team composition with implications for research and practice.

Johnson, J., & Traylor, A. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Proactive personality and team conflict: The moderating role of constructive controversy training in academic research teams.

Nguyen, Q., Nguyen, J., Rousseau, M., Grossman, R., & Feitosa, J. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Temporal and dyadic perspectives on diversity, trust, and performance.

Despite decades of research on team composition, I-Os still lack consistent answers about how to assemble people into effective teams. This symposium goes beyond traditional approaches. Authors examine how personality dispersion influences conflict over time and whether training interventions help, explore temporal dynamics showing how surface and deep-level diversity operate differently than previously thought, and introduce teammate expectations as a missing link between traits and outcomes. These studies offer fresh perspectives on team composition with implications for research and practice.

title: The Personal and Organizational Costs of Unhelpful and Imbalanced Support at Work (Session ID 1127)
tracks: Occupational Health/Work and Family | Content Area Theme Track

speaker_affiliations: University of Tennessee at Chattanooga | University Of Tennessee At Chattanooga | University of New Mexico | Bowling Green State University

description: Black, K. (Co-Chair), Lamprecht, J. (Co-Chair) & Hetrick, A. (Discussant) (2026, April 30 – May 2). The personal and organizational costs of unhelpful and imbalanced support at work [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Lamprecht, J. & Black, K (2026, April 30 – May 2). More than an annoyance: Unhelpful workplace support and worker loneliness. Gray, C. E., Skovera, I. R., DiGiovanni, A. M, Spector, P. E., Wells, J. E., & Hughes, I. M. (2026, April 30 - May 2). Just as harmful as abusive supervision? Outcomes of unhelpful support from supervisors. Rohr, S. K., Barratt, C. L., & Warner, B. M. (2026, April 30 – May 2). When helping hurts: The behavioral impact of support imbalance. Social support is intuitively beneficial, but the research around support in the workplace is complex and inconsistent. Social support that is actually unhelpful (e.g., undermining confidence, unwanted) or imbalanced can even have negative effects. Three papers highlight nuances of how unhelpful social support can affect workers, increasing feelings of loneliness, depriving workers of core psychological needs, and reducing engagement. In addition, authors present how imbalanced support relates to fewer organizational citizenship behaviors and more counterproductive workplace behaviors.

Practicing What We Preach: Integrating “I” & “O” Psychology in Applied Fieldwork [IGNITE]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session will address the traditionally distinct “I” and “O” sides of the field, in which the I-side has historically focused on the assessment and measurement of KSAOs, and the O-side has focused on the context of assessment and measurement in relation to the social, emotional, and health consequences of individuals. Presenters will provide applied examples of how the traditionally “distinct” sides of our field are heavily integrated through their work. This IGNITE! session highlights the interconnection between the “I” and “O” in practice, showing how insights from both sides are used by practitioners to address workplace challenges.

High Impact HiPo programs in Large Enterprises: Evidence, Practice, & Future Trends. [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. High-potential (HiPo) programs remain one of the most widely used and debated talent strategies in large organizations. They promise to secure leadership pipelines, accelerate business impact, and strengthen engagement. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. The past year has brought major federal deregulatory-focused changes, and states have responded with new AI laws. AI-focused litigation continues, exposing both vendors and users to legal risk. Navigating these evolving regulations and challenges in AI-based employee selection remains difficult. This session will provide updates on federal, state, and international regulations and recent case law. This is the third iteration of this session at SIOP. Attendees will leave with a thorough understanding of the legal landscape, with a focus on updates between the SIOP 2025 and 2026 conferences.

title: Modeling Mindsets for Modern Organizations: Cultivating Statistical Thinking in I-O (Session ID 941)

description: Summers, D. & Courey, K. A. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Modeling Mindsets for Modern Organizations: Cultivating Statistical Thinking in I-O [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Statistical training remains central to I-O psychology, yet most curricula still rely on null hypothesis significance testing (NHST). This session advocates a model-based approach, positioning the general linear model as the conceptual foundation for teaching, research, and practice. Participants will explore how this framework unifies statistical methods, fosters deeper reasoning, and aligns with the data-rich demands of modern organizations. An interactive CPD exercise will guide attendees in developing actionable plans to enhance model-based fluency.

title: Partner Showcase: Pinsight - AI is Ready. Are your Leaders? What 14,000 Leadership Assessments Reveal About Why AI Adoption Stalls
speakers: Martin Lanik | Amy Huber
speaker_affiliations: Pinsight
speaker_titles: CEO | Vice President, Client Success
description: Organizations are investing heavily in Artificial Intelligence (AI), yet many struggle to move beyond pilots and demonstrations to achieve enterprise-scale impact. Most conversations focus on technology and data readiness, but our research suggests a different constraint: leadership capability. Drawing on a global sample of 14,000 leaders assessed through behavioral simulations and personality measures, this session examines how leadership readiness for AI varies across senior executives, mid-level leaders, and frontline managers. The findings reveal a consistent pattern: organizations can define AI strategy but struggle to translate it into coordinated execution at each level. Senior executives often face challenges leading large-scale transformation, mid-level leaders struggle to translate AI strategy into operational change, and frontline managers lack the skills to guide workforce adoption. By highlighting patterns across industries and regions, attendees will leave with a data-driven view of what leadership capabilities must shift to turn AI investment into sustained business impact.
description_html: Organizations are investing heavily in Artificial Intelligence (AI), yet many struggle to move beyond pilots and demonstrations to achieve enterprise-scale impact. Most conversations focus on technology and data readiness, but our research suggests a different constraint: leadership capability. Drawing on a global sample of 14,000 leaders assessed through behavioral simulations and personality measures, this session examines how leadership readiness for AI varies across senior executives, mid-level leaders, and frontline managers. The findings reveal a consistent pattern: organizations can define AI strategy but struggle to translate it into coordinated execution at each level. Senior executives often face challenges leading large-scale transformation, mid-level leaders struggle to translate AI strategy into operational change, and frontline managers lack the skills to guide workforce adoption. By highlighting patterns across industries and regions, attendees will leave with a data-driven view of what leadership capabilities must shift to turn AI investment into sustained business impact.

Bowling, N. A., Lyons, B. D, Sexton, D., Mullaney, K., & Wood, D. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Developing and Validating a Context-Specific CWB Scale in the U.S. Military. Mercado, B. K., Dilchert, S., & Ones, D. S. (2026, April 30 – May 2). That escalated quickly! Early police misconduct predicts severe outcomes. Nguyen, K. M., Rodriguez, W. A., Zhang, H., Zhou, Z. E. Unique features of the academic counterproductive work behavior ecosystem. Counterproductive work behavior (CWB) research has often treated context (e.g., occupation or industry) as incidental. This symposium addresses that limitation by examining CWB across diverse settings, including the military, law enforcement, nursing, and academia. Presenters will illustrate how context shapes the nature, antecedents, and consequences of CWB, revealing both commonalities and unique patterns. Collectively, these insights highlight the theoretical and practical value of situating CWB within the work environment.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Employees spend a lot of time in meetings, with organizations questioning whether they drive value or drain productivity. Scholars have highlighted the costs of poor quality meetings, whereas organizations like Shopify have gone so far as to cancel meetings to reset norms, with new technology like AI expanding the possibilities. This session will bring together meetings experts and I-O practitioners to share research and real-world examples of interventions that improved meeting quality, followed by small-group discussions where participants will propose their own interventions.

How susceptible are open-ended personality assessments to faking and the use of GenAI. Increased accessibility to generative artificial intelligence (AI)-based tools make it possible for candidates to leverage these tools in unproctored prehire assessments. It is unclear from research whether and how this will impact validity and quality of hire. The 5 projects in this symposium aim to sharpen focus on the impact of generative (AI) in selection assessments by examining impact on validity and differences in responses when AI is used, and evaluating potential ways to either reduce applicants’ opportunity to use these tools or more effectively detect it when they do.

Sylvara, A., Sargent, M., Wang, P., Heron, X., & Sun, T. (2026, April 30 – May 2). This is Me Trying… to Fake using AI: Examination of Human Faking and AI-Assisted Faking on an AI Chatbot Personality Interview.
Chawota, T., & Speer, A. B. (2026, April 30 – May 2). How susceptible are open-ended personality assessments to faking and the use of GenAI.
 

 

Increased accessibility to generative artificial intelligence (AI)-based tools make it possible for candidates to leverage these tools in unproctored prehire assessments. It is unclear from research whether and how this will impact validity and quality of hire. The 5 projects in this symposium aim to sharpen focus on the impact of generative (AI) in selection assessments by examining impact on validity and differences in responses when AI is used, and evaluating potential ways to either reduce applicants’ opportunity to use these tools or more effectively detect it when they do.

title: Fear and Loathing in Leadership Research (Session ID 681)

description: Miller, C. F. (Co-Chair), Hoffman, B. J. (Co-Chair), Dietz, J. (Panelist), Gottfredson, R. (Panelist), Matta, F. (Panelist), Narghang, J. (Panelist), & Zaccaro, S. (Panelist). (2026, April 30 – May 2). Fear and Loathing in Leadership Research: Where We Are Now and Where We Need to Go [Panel Discussion]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Leadership research is currently undergoing a period of fear and loathing, similar to that described in Hunter Thompson’s classic 1970s novel. In recent years, leadership research has been widely critiqued for its lack of theoretical cohesion and methodological shortcomings. Yet, although many have raised problems with leadership research, the path forward is unclear. This session addresses these concerns by bringing together some of the field's most accomplished leadership scholars to summarize the current problems with leadership research and provide a vision for how they can be addressed.

tracks: Selection/Assessment/Individual Differences | Content Area Theme Track

speaker_affiliations: Talogy | Delta Air Lines | U.S. Customs and Border Protection | Dunnette Group | The Nancy T. Tippins Group, LLC | University of Minnesota
speaker_titles: General Manager, Talent Assessment & Strategy | Personnel Research Psychologist | Vice President, Consulting Practice, Americas | President | Principal
description: Lambert, D. D. (Chair), Abraham, J. D., Cucina, J. M., Hough, L. M., Sackett, P. R., Solomonson, A., & Tippins, N. T. (2026). Cognitive Ability in Personnel Selection: What Is Its Place Today? [Panel Discussion]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Recent meta-analytic research examining cognitive ability and job performance, focused on data from contemporary studies, has produced validity estimates far lower than the traditionally accepted value of .51 produced by Schmidt and Hunter (1998). Researchers agree, however, that regardless of one’s perspective on the magnitude of the estimate, cognitive ability continues to play an important role in the overall world of personnel selection. Panelists will discuss perspectives from research and practice regarding when and how cognitive ability may best be utilized in selection systems today.

speakers: Allison Besl | Hannah Markell-Goldstein | Jordan Kirkland | Justin D. Purl | Carson Sandy

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Industrial-organizational (I-O) psychologists are well-positioned to close the gap between analysis and action in People Analytics. This session highlights projects where insights were translated into structural change: redesigning succession planning to emphasize readiness and accountability, using predictive models to guide stock-based retention, rebuilding leadership frameworks with behavioral clarity, tailoring dashboards to decision-making, and standardizing quality-of-hire metrics. title: Board Characteristics' Diverse Impacts on Sustainability (Session ID 987)
speakers: Stephan Dilchert | Phoebe Hessen | Sydney Higley | Yagizhan Yazar
speaker_affiliations: Zicklin School of Business | University of Minnesota | College of Liberal Arts, University of Minnesota Twin Cities | Tapestry Inc.
speaker_titles: Undergraduate Research Assistant | Senior Associate, DEI
description: Dilchert, S. (Co-Chair & Discussant), Ren, Z. (Non-Speaking Co-Chair), & Ones, D.S. (Non-Speaking Co-Chair). (2026, April 30 - May 2). Board Characteristics' Diverse Impacts on Sustainability [Sympoisum]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conferene, New Orleans, LA, United States. Four studies analyzing several thousand organizations worldwide indicate that board gender diversity enhances resource conservation and stakeholder engagement but not transformative innovation or harm prevention. Furthermore, these effects reverse across cultures: board gender diversity predicts greater sustainability in Asia and Anglo countries but reduced sustainability in Latin Europe. Larger boards enhance change-oriented initiatives but not compliance behaviors. CEO duality shows negligible effects. These findings provide guidance for organizations designing boards to embed social and environmental sustainability.

This master tutorial focuses on the use of data visualization to properly communicate information and persuade public audiences. Generative artificial intelligence in workplace assessment development: Pros and cons [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology, New Orleans, LA, United States. Generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) holds promise for many research and practical applications in workplace settings, but remains understudied in many important fields such as workplace psychological assessment. In this symposium, we demonstrate the use of GenAI to develop and categorize multiple item types for 6 psychological constructs, discuss the advantages and limitations of GenAI in developing and validating psychological assessment items for the workplace, potential next steps and, in lieu of a discussant, reserve ample time for audience discussion.

title: Partner Showcase: Deloitte - Work Design in the Age of Humans x Machines: AI Value Realization
speakers: Sarah Szpaichler Stough
speaker_affiliations: Deloitte Consulting
speaker_titles: Work Engineering Leader
description: Organizations are increasing AI investment, yet many aren’t seeing expected returns because technology-first strategies are under-emphasizing the human advantage, a key driver of competitive differentiation. This session explores an approach to Humans × Machines collaboration including how to orchestrate unique human skills and machine capability to deliver exponential outcomes. Grounded in tipping points highlighted in Deloitte’s 2026 Global Human Capital Trends , we’ll share activities that accelerate value realization through Work Design Engineering: clear outcomes, trustworthy decisioning, data/process readiness, adoption by design, and intentional work design. We’ll include a brief visualization of Deloitte’s Work Design Canvas to illustrate how teams re-balance work across automation, augmentation, and uniquely human strengths.

speakers: Claire Smith | Sarah Rohr | Julie Wayne | Luiza Flores | Savannah Flak | Shaun Pichler | Barbara Warner
speaker_affiliations: University of South Florida | Bowling Green State University | Wake Forest University | Colorado State University | Human Resource Management (HRM)

description: Rohr, S. K. (Co-Chair) & Smith, C. E. (Co-Chair) (2026, April 30 – May 2). New perspectives on work-family support [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Flak, S. R., Smith, C. E., & Barratt, C. L. (2026, April 30 – May 2). The structure of workplace support networks: Implications of support exchange relationship and network profiles for work-family conflict. Flores, L. C., Cortez, J., & French, K. A. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Work-family experience of single parents: A systematic literature review. Pichler, S., Xu, W., & Yu, A. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Supervisor support, work-family conflict, and citizenship behavior: A test of competing theoretical perspectives. Warner, B. M., & Barratt, C. L. (2026, April 30 – May 2). The Impact of Giving and Receiving Support on the Work-Family Domain. Work–family support remains a pressing issue for organizations hoping to protect and retain employees and, therefore, also for organizational researchers working to describe and explain it. Across 4 diverse projects (i.e., curvilinear effects, competing longitudinal mediations, social network considerations, a review of research on a special population) integrated by an expert discussant (Dr. Julie Wayne), the symposium reframes support as more than a simple direct predictor or buffer of work–family outcomes—instead as a complex, exchange-based, and sometimes demanding phenomenon. Work–family support remains a pressing issue for organizations hoping to protect and retain employees and, therefore, also for organizational researchers working to describe and explain it. Across 4 diverse projects (i.e., curvilinear effects, competing longitudinal mediations, social network considerations, a review of research on a special population) integrated by an expert discussant (Dr. Julie Wayne), the symposium reframes support as more than a simple direct predictor or buffer of work–family outcomes—instead as a complex, exchange-based, and sometimes demanding phenomenon. Authors will discuss new insights for measuring, theorizing, and intervening when supporting work–family issues.

Adaptive Team Coordination Patterns: The Role of Leadership and Event Disruptiveness in Shaping Team Coordination Coordination is a dynamic, adaptive process essential for teamwork, yet much research relies on static, cross-sectional approaches. This symposium presents 5 papers that move beyond snapshots to capture coordination as it unfolds. Contributions include qualitative insights from adversarial contexts, a 2-dimensional framework for planning vs real-time coordination, neural network modeling of multimodal data, validation of objective trace metrics, and studies on leadership and disruptive events. Together, these papers advance theory, methods, and assessment strategies for team coordination.

Navigating cleft habitus: A grounded theory study of upward class transitioners in professional workplaces. Traditional leadership frameworks have perpetuated a narrowly defined leader prototype (typically White, male, higher social class), resulting in the exclusion and undervaluation of the unique strengths held by leaders with marginalized identities. This symposium challenges such restrictive narratives about who is an ideal leader by first examining the cognitive and relational mechanisms through which these biases manifest in organizational settings, and then reorienting the conversation around the inherent value, agency, and unique strengths that arise from experiences navigating the margins.

Beyond Burnout: Value Violations, Shame, and Moral Injury in Everyday Organizations. Glasgow, T.E., Benishek, L., & DiazGranados, D. (2026). Moral Injury Among Emergency Department Nurses. Cortez, J. & Fletcher, K.A. (2026). Organizational Dehumanization Differentially Impacts Exchange Quality with All Levels of the Organization. This symposium examines moral injury as a values-based trauma mechanism linking organizational failures to employee harm. Authors show that moral transgressions and organizational dehumanization function as potentially morally injurious events. These events elicit destructive moral emotions (e.g., shame), which, when amplified by psychological proximity or an antiwork orientation, accelerate strain and reduce organizational commitment. These studies provide novel, evidence-based mechanisms for researchers and practitioners to address moral hazards embedded in contemporary work life.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This 80-minute interview explores how organizations can align AI adoption with UN SDGs for 2030/2050. Dr. Romella Janene and Dr. Zonke Zungu compare U.S. deregulation with South Africa’s equity-driven approach, identifying shared principles and adaptive strategies. description: Thomas, K. N. (Co-Chair), Bajwa, N. (Co-Chair), Cortina, J., Litson, K., Simone, M. & Zhang, B. (2026, April 30 – May 2). lavaan Will Fit It, But Should We? Rethinking Fit, Judgment, and Responsibility in SEM [Panel] Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States Structural equation modeling (SEM) is a common tool in organizational science because it unites theory, latent variables, and complex designs in one framework. Yet its popularity can come with recurring problems: weak reporting, overfit “pretty” models, cross-sectional designs interpreted casually and thin handling of missing data and power. This panel brings methodologists and applied researchers together to discuss (a) where SEM uniquely advances organizational theory (b) common missteps and pragmatic fixes and (c) additional design and argument clarification for causal claims and replication.

Beyond Checkboxes: Practicing Intersectionality Through Role-playing and Reflexivity [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States Intersectionality is often invoked but rarely practiced with rigor. This 80-minute interactive alternative session moves beyond definitions to applied skill-building and provides participants with practical post-session guidance, prompts, and references via a 1-page toolkit. Focusing on the core tenets of intersectionality, authors will guide a live identity mapping activity and facilitate a scenario lab with multiple cases that require participants to diagnose intersectional blind spots and choose evidence-based actions to resolve them.

Examining the Efficacy of Large Language Models for Thematic Content Analysis. Although capturing conversations within teams offer a fruitful avenue for understanding team dynamics, this type of data collection strategy yields large, complex data sets that can be difficult and time-consuming to manage. Recent advancements in AI have created opportunities for different analytic techniques as well as removing barriers to managing larger qualitative data sets. This symposium features 4 projects that highlight strategies for using AI in analysis of teams data. Importantly, authors end with a discussion with the former JOB editor on ethical considerations of using AI in research.

speakers: Naidan Tu | Stephen Stark | Jinsoo Choi | Lavanya S. Kumar | Philseok Lee | Xavier Heron
speaker_affiliations: Kansas State University | University of South Florida | University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign | Graduate Management Admission Council | George Mason University | Rice University

description: Tu, N. (Co-Chair) & Stark, S. (Co-Chair). (2026, April 30-May 2). Forced Choice Testing in Noncognitive Assessment: New Methodologies and Technologies [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Noncognitive constructs such as personality play a key role in predicting workplace outcomes and reducing hiring biases. This symposium highlights recent technological and methodological advancements in noncognitive assessment, with a focus on forced choice (FC) testing. The studies included address several key areas: testing assumptions underlying FC test construction and scoring, introducing new FC models to improve the accuracy and precision of noncognitive measurement, and exploring natural language processing (NLP) as an alternative approach to traditional IRT for scoring FC measures.

Walking a Tightrope: Navigating the line between personal expression and professionalism. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel explores how employees navigate the tension between personal expression and professionalism in increasingly diverse workplaces. Panelists will address strategies such as code-switching and masking, the influence of organizational norms like professionalism and the “ideal worker,” and the boundaries employees draw between personal and professional life. Together, the discussion highlights the need to challenge existing norms, broaden boundary research beyond work–family, and identify future directions for inclusive organizational practices.

description: Headrick, L. (Co-Chair), Wang, Z. (Co-Chair) & Rudolph, C.W. (Discussant) (2026, April 30 – May 2). Shaping work in the modern era: Expanding perspectives on job crafting across contexts. [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. As contemporary work is transformed by technological innovation, evolving identities, and heightened performance demands, employees increasingly engage in job crafting to align their work with personal and organizational needs. This symposium integrates 3 papers examining generative AI use in job crafting, relational crafting amid stigmatized identity management, and demands crafting under accountability. Collectively, these studies advance theoretical understanding of job crafting as a mechanism of proactive adaptation, self-regulation, and meaningful work construction in dynamic contexts.

description: McClure, J., Bupp, C., Holcombe, K., Menendez, J., & Wallace, L. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Future-ready leaders: Build them or buy them? [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. As businesses navigate accelerated change, the concept of "future-ready leadership" has emerged as a critical capability. This panel explores a fundamental question facing talent management professionals: Can future-ready leadership competencies be developed through targeted interventions, or do they represent relatively stable individual differences that organizations must identify and select for? Panelists will draw from research on leadership development, personality psychology, and change management to reach a conclusion on whether future-ready leadership should be bought or built.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Qualitative methods are innovative and underused tools for I-O psychologists to address organizational challenges in both research and practice. They are especially valuable for capturing employee experiences and uncovering key processes, though their use often involves trade-offs due to organizational constraints. description: Battista, M., Martin, M., Wu, J., Tavis, A., Cox, G., Feinzig, S. (2026, April 30-May 2). The Future of Management Development. Panel Discussion. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. The world of work has greatly shifted and with that so has the role of a manager. It is no longer enough to manage workflow, achieve results through others, gather resources, and develop team members. Today managers also need to navigate complex challenges like cost pressures, dwindling team morale and the relentless need for organizational growth. And yet this still does not fully describe what managers face on a daily basis. This panel will discuss what the future of management development will entail and end with a call to action for researchers and practitioners.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Authors discuss a new measure of corporate quality of hire developed and validated using publicly available data on new hire outcomes and corporate financial performance. They hear from 3 organizations with exceptionally high scores on this metric. The panel concludes with insights from a leading researcher on methods for capturing new hire value.

description: Jackson, C.L. & Ulrich, R.M. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Standardizing Excellence: Change Strategies for High-Skill Environments [Master Tutorial]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. In companies where craftsmanship, pride, and autonomy are deeply embedded in the culture, introducing standard work creates a paradox: driving consistency without losing the artistry that defines the work. This Master Tutorial, for intermediate to advanced audiences, explores that tension through Gibson Guitars, a legacy manufacturer known for artisanal quality and long-standing tradition of excellence. Attendees will gain a people-centered framework for aligning operational excellence with culture to drive behavior change and consistency in technically complex, high-skill environments.

description: DeKoekkoek, P. (Chair), Mueller, L. (Discussant). (2026). Applications of Different Modifications of the Angoff Approach for Cut Score Setting [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Nugent, S., Zhang, X., Li, I., & Myers, T. (2026). Applying the Rasch Measurement Theory to Evaluate Judgments in Standard Setting. Reddock, C. & Tomeh, D. (2026). Setting Score Bands for a Technical Assessment Using Angoff Ratings. Lin, Y., Beazley, C., & DeKoekkoek, P. (2026). From Complex Models to Clear Decisions: Proficiency Banding in IRT-Based Assessments of Behavioral Skills. With an increased focus on skills, criterion-referenced cut scores set using the Angoff approach offer a way for organizations to make hiring decisions based on whether a candidate has a skill. The Angoff approach has limitations. This symposium discusses modifications to the Angoff approach to address different limitations like unexplained variance in ratings and SMEs underestimating the difficulty of items. The approach can also be modified for soft skills. This session will be informative to individuals unfamiliar with criterion-referenced cut score setting and experienced practitioners.

This panel will present research and applied experience on how artificial intelligence currently impacts disability inclusion in the workplace. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. How much do you really know about evaluating learning and development? In this interactive, game-show-style session, participants will test their knowledge of training evaluation myths. Using live polling, we’ll challenge common misconceptions about training evaluation methods, models and metrics, and share research that refutes them. description: King, K., Lopez, P., Baker-Salmon, Z., Barnes, A. N., Cox, B., Drexler, J. & Campana, K.L. (Chair) (2026, April 30 – May 2). Whose Classroom Is It Anyway? Reflecting on Do’s and Don’ts of Experiential Learning [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel explores the Classroom-as-Organization model, an experiential learning approach in which courses are structured to function like organizations. Student panelists who participated in a graduate-level course will share first-hand reflections on the benefits, challenges, and lessons learned from this method. By discussing both “do’s” and “don’ts,” the session provides practical guidance, highlights common pitfalls, and encourages faculty to consider how this approach can enrich teaching and learning.

title: A Panel About Panels: New Trends in Crowdsourced Research Panels (Session ID 398)
speakers: Manuel Gonzalez | I-Heng (Ray) Wu | Aaron Moss | Bradley Pitcher | Melissa Keith | Richard Landers | Louis Tay
speaker_affiliations: Montclair State University | University of New Mexico- Anderson School of Management | CloudResearch | APTMetrics | Bowling Green State University | Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology (APA Div #14) | Purdue University

A Panel About Panels: New Trends in Crowdsourced Research Panels [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Crowdsourced research platforms have gained prominence in organizational research. However, both the research landscape and technologies are continually evolving, which may in various ways impact how I-Os should use these platforms to ensure data quality. A panel of 5 researchers—representing academia, industry, and internal platform work—is convened to discuss what new opportunities and challenges may emerge with crowdsourcing platforms. In doing so, the session will contribute to continued, creative, and rigorous research using these platforms.

(2026, April 30 – May 2). Words at Work: Applications and Best Practices of Archival Text Data in I-O Psychology [IGNITE]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. A data explosion in the past decade has created opportunities for I-O psychologists to leverage archival text as a data source for research. Archival text research draws on data originally collected for reasons other than answering a researcher's current research question. In I-O psychology, such sources range from job reviews to work emails to social media posts. These sources offer unique strengths and weaknesses. In this session, authors will showcase how I-Os have used these sources to study work, highlighting opportunities for future research and providing guidance for navigating limitations.

Immigrants in the Workplace: New Directions for Research and Practice [Alternative Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Immigrants remain an overlooked population in I-O psychology, despite being vital to the global workforce. This session brings together experts studying immigrant, refugee, and undocumented worker experiences to discuss research on barriers to employment and equity. After brief presentations on the state of the science, attendees will participate in breakout discussions to generate research questions for advancing immigrant inclusion in organizations. This session will serve as an introduction to immigrant research and a space for scholars to generate new ideas and develop new collaborations.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This interactive working session invites attendees to directly shape how SIOP supports its practitioner community, with a critical focus on early career practitioners who are known to be at a higher risk of attrition. The session will begin with a brief overview of the current efforts from SIOP’s Professional Practice Committee. The remaining time will be dedicated to small group discussions where attendees are invited to share their suggestions on what they need and want from SIOP. Insights gathered will be synthesized and shared with the Executive Board to directly inform future strategy.

title: Product Showcase: Deloitte - Agentic Change: Using AI to Personalize Adoption and Elevate Human Leadership
speakers: Chloe Domergue
speaker_affiliations: Deloitte
speaker_titles: Principal & US Strategic & Technology Change Eminence & Innovation Lead
description: Organizations invest in transformation, yet adoption stalls because change approaches are static and disconnected from how people work. Domergue’s work introduces a “new change paradigm” and an agentic change model that embeds AI agents into the flow of work to deliver personalized nudges and adoption interventions grounded in behavioral science. These agent networks detect friction points, tailor support to motivation and context, and adapt based on response. The goal is not to replace change leaders, but to augment them. By automating behavioral interventions at scale, agentic systems free practitioners to focus on higher-value work: aligning leaders, strengthening relationship capital, shaping employee experience, and making context-sensitive decisions. Use cases will be woven into the dialogue, showing application in regulated environments while preserving trust, engagement, and psychological safety. Rooted in I-O principles such as reinforcement and job design, this approach reframes change as a human–AI partnership designed to build adaptive organizations.

title: Invisible Strains, Real Consequences: Breaking Barriers to Support Vulnerable Workers (Session ID 416)
speakers: Jacquelyn Brady | Shalene Allen | Hoda Vaziri | Jazmin Argueta-Rivera | Kaushik Ram | Marian Hernandez

description: Allen, S. J. (Co-Chair), Brady, J. M. (Co-Chair), & Smith, N. A. (Discussant) (2026, April 30 – May 2). Invisible strains, real consequences: Breaking barriers to support vulnerable workers [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Workers experiencing vulnerability often face “invisible strains” rooted in stigma, marginalization, and personal hardship, leading to tangible consequences. This symposium examines how diverse forms of vulnerability influence workers’ experiences and how organizations can either mitigate or exacerbate harm. Together, these papers deepen understanding of how strains emerge and highlight the need to foster inclusive, safe, and supportive workplaces through theoretical and practical insights focused on creating a more equitable workforce.

This session will highlight the application of industrial/organizational (I-O) psychology to elite sports organizations. Panelists will discuss the unique context of working in elite sports, covering topics that range from working with elite athletes and coaches “on the field” to working “in the business” with front-office and other organizational staff. description: Kell, H. J. (Co-Chair), & Martin-Raugh, M. P. (Co-Chair). (2025). Situational judgment test innovations by and for humans: No AI required [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Amid growing enthusiasm for artificial intelligence (AI) in assessment, this symposium highlights 5 advances in situational judgment testing that demonstrate the enduring value of human expertise. Each presentation illustrates how theory-driven design, empirical evidence, and psychological insight continue to advance assessment quality. The papers address construct fidelity, contextual validity, faking mitigation, candidate experience, and large-scale implementation, all without relying on AI. Together they show that meaningful innovation arises from thoughtful science, not automation.

description: Kaiser, R. B. (Chair) (2026). Leadership and the Human Capital Advantage in Private Equity [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Private equity (PE) is a huge and growing industry with over $15 trillion in assets under management globally. PE firms buy and grow companies to increase their value and sell them for a profit. It is an intensely competitive industry, with few ways for firms to differentiate themselves. However, one source of competitive advantage concerns human capital and talent management. This session features presentations that illustrate the application of the science of talent management and development, focused on leadership, in PE-backed companies.

title: Being Significant With Nonsignificant Data (Session ID 1151)
speakers: Leann Caudill | Melanie Standish | Ron Landis
speaker_affiliations: Coastal Carolina University | Lenovo | Wilbur O. and Ann Powers College of Business at Clemson University · Full-time
speaker_titles: Assistant Professor | Senior Leadership & Organization Development Specialist | Clinical Associate Professor
description: Caudill, L. E. (Co-Chair), Standish, M. P. (Co-Chair), & Landis R. S. (Discussant). (2026, April 30 – May 2). Being significant with nonsignificant data. [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. I-Os spend countless hours theorizing and designing research studies in hopes of shedding light on interesting workplace phenomena. But what happens when results are “nonsignificant?” I-Os might need to think outside the box because contributions can still be significant. This alternative session will provide numerous examples of how to move forward with nonsignificant data. Attendees will organize into small discussion groups for most of the session to share experiences with null results to find paths forward, in hopes of fostering collaboration and accountability.

speakers: Ami Shah | Manuel Gonzalez | Nikitha Mohan | Paul Spector | Heewon Jang | Renee Leger | Sara Stavely

description: Gonzalez, M. F. (Co-Chair), Mohan, N. S. (Co-Chair), Shah, A. (Co-Chair), & Spector, P. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Beyond the episode: Considering enduring forms of emotions at work [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Jang, H., Kaplan, S., Kuykendall, L., Liao, X., & Maguire, L. (2026, April 30 – May 2). When the Weekend Ends: How Sunday Affective Thoughts Shape Monday Work. Stavely, S. M., & Gray, E. C. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Grief at Work: A Systematic Review of Bereavement and Organizational Support. Leger, R., & Cohen-Charash, Y. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Forgiving and Forgetting? Easier Said than Done: Hate as a Sentiment at Work Research on emotions has continued to be prominent over the last few decades; however, most of it focuses on episodic emotions, moods, and affective traits. This symposium highlights 3 research papers investigating affect, extending beyond brief episodes and dispositional traits. The papers explore emotional spillover to new contexts, transformation of short-term emotions into chronic affective patterns, and the conceptualization of emotions as sentiments. Collectively, the current symposium illustrates exciting new research avenues for studying affect in organizations.

description: Jackson, C.L., Elick, J. A., McManus, A., Ulrich, R.M. (2026, April 30 - May2). Pressure-Tested Engagement: What Keeps Manufacturing Workers Connected [Master Tutorial]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Manufacturing workforces face unique engagement challenges, particularly when under pressure. This tutorial shares findings from GP Strategies’ engagement data and a Gibson Guitars case study to reveal what drives and sustains engagement when stress peaks. Intermediate-level attendees will explore key drivers of engagement and resilience, discover scalable strategies for front-line workers, and examine how embedded, institutionalized systems reinforce performance within an industrial workforce. The session bridges data, practice, and narrative psychology to drive lasting impact.

title: LLMs-Based Methods for More Reliable and Efficient Structured Interviews (Session ID 744)
speakers: Georgi Yankov | Josh Liff | David Mayers | Isaac Thompson | Zihan Yan
speaker_affiliations: Hogan Assessments | HireVue | Knockri | Amazon | University of illinois

description: Yankov, G. P. (Chair) (2026). LLMs-based methods for more reliable and efficient structured interviews [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Structured interviews are strong predictors of job performance but exhibit substantial variability in validity. This symposium examines how large language models (LLMs) can reduce this variability by enhancing score reliability, optimizing interview length, and ensuring raters provide accurate, evidence-based feedback. The 4 studies address sources of rater noise, the relationship between interview length and its reliability, an IRT-inspired method for shortening interviews, and automated tools for verifying interviewer debriefs.

description: Petery, G. A. (Chair). (2026). Rethinking Retirement: HRM Retention Strategies for Sustaining Work Ability [IGNITE+ Panel Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session explores how longer lifespans and shifting demographics are reshaping retirement. Presenters examine outdated “cliff” retirement models, phased retirement policies, retention strategies for older workers, and work ability as a key late-career metric. Together, they highlight how organizations can design flexible policies and practices that retain talent, support well-being, and promote successful transitions to retirement.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This symposium examines artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) methods for optimizing personnel selection, measurement validation, and taxonomy development. Three studies showcase how these technologies deliver reliable and valid results with greater efficiency and scalability compared to conventional applied statistical approaches. The session features cutting-edge examples of empirically grounded and ethically responsible human-in-the-loop AI/ML integration in people analytics for intermediate to advanced audiences.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Moral and ethical challenges are a prevalent source of workplace stress that harm well-being, negatively impact job attitudes, and predict employee attrition. This panel of experts will discuss what moral harm is, its effects on employees, and how organizations and I-O psychologists can address related challenges. The goal is to build the audience's understanding of the nature and dynamics of these challenges in the workplace and how they may be addressed.

Swanson, A., Linnell, A., Laverghetta Jr., A., Luchini, S., Beaty, R., & Reiter-Palmon, R. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Old tests, new tricks: Using LLMs to expand creativity assessment items Boira-Lopez, A., Theobald E., D’Amato, A., Hunter S., Rygg, J., & Elson, J. (2026, April 30 – May 2). When Generative AI helps and when it hurts: Sequencing and levels of analysis in creative problem solving. d’Amato, A. L., Theobald, E., Rygg, J., Elson, J. S., & Hunter, S. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Outside the guardrails: A dual-study exploration of AI-enhanced malevolent creativity. Creativity researchers are currently contending with whether and how large language models (LLMs) inform what I-Os know about human creativity. This symposium moves beyond the question of whether generative AI can be creative. Across 4 studies, the authors examine questions of whether human and AI creativity can be differentiated, how to leverage LLMs to assess creativity and enhance creative problem solving, and what guardrails should be in place considering the potential for malevolent creativity.

title: Demonstrating the Use of Escape Rooms as a Novel Research Method (Session ID 391)
speakers: Takudzwa Chawota | Nissa Gongora | Steven Zhou
speaker_affiliations: Wayne State University | Claremont McKenna College
speaker_titles: Graduate Teaching Assistant | Assistant Professor
description: Zhou, S., Chawota, T. A., Gongora, N., & Dickson, M. W. (2026, April 29 – May 2). Demonstrating the use of escape rooms as a novel research method [Alternative session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This alternative session provides a live, interactive demonstration of an escape room used as a novel research method for data collection on topics related to leadership, teams, motivation, and strategic planning. Escape rooms, which became popular in recent years, are real-life adventure games where teams of participants work together to solve puzzles in order to “escape” a simulated game. Authors argue that escape rooms offer a controlled environment to test I-O-related theories while maximizing participant engagement in a way that will attract people to participate in research studies.

description: Beazley, C. (Chair), Bennett, M., de Klerk, G., M., Fetzer, Lin, Y., Porr, B., Rutherford, K. (2026, April 30 – May 2) Text to talent– Practical applications of LLMs for evaluating constructed responses [IGNITE]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Although the use of Large Language Models (LLMs) has exploded across industries, using LLMs for evaluating responses is a novel approach in talent assessment and talent development. There are open questions around the accuracy, fairness, and quality of the approach. This introductory IGNITE session showcases practical applications of using LLMs for evaluating a range of constructed response types. Presenters will outline real world case studies that investigate the accuracy, reliability, explainability, security, and fairness of LLM evaluation.

description: Liu, M. (Co-Chair), & Hickman, L. (Co-Chair). (2026). Machine learning for I-O 8.0: Large Language Models for Assessments [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Nakamoto, S., Lee, V., Cavanaugh, K. J., Bach-Lefler, I., & Landers, R. N. (2026). Multimodal LLM assessment of personality and job performance from social media. Li, W., McNeney, D., Barr, R., Prabhu, N., Zhang, C., Capman, J. F., & Boyce, A. S. (2026). Identifying requisition-specific knowledge, skills, and abilities with large language models. Wang, P., Hou, D., Sun, T., & Tay, L. (2026). Investigating the psychometric properties of multimodal large language model-based performance evaluation. Stevenor, B., Anyanwu, C., & McBride, N. L. (2026). Predicting item difficulty with AI. Nie, W. & Dabdoub, A. (2026). Less is more? Testing single-item and AI-based predictors of personality in short video interviews. The emergence of large language models (LLMs) presents many opportunities for industrial-organizational psychology research and practice. However, empirical research is limited regarding the capacity of LLMs to conduct various assessment-related tasks. This symposium presents 5 papers that research and apply LLMs to core problems of scoring constructs and scale development. The papers provide nuanced validity evidence for using LLMs to assess personality and vocational interests, extract competencies from job requisitions, and predict item difficulty.

speakers: Carolyn Kalafut | Evan Theys | Justin D. Purl | Kate LaPort | Nathan Hundley | Nicholas Martin

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. As AI transforms people analytics, the role of I-O psychologists is increasingly complex and critical. This panel features experts who’ve led AI initiatives across organizations, discussing how I-O psychologists should be embedded in AI development to protect data integrity, uphold ethics, and ensure models reflect human behavior. Real-world examples will highlight successful collaborations with technical teams and the value of cross-disciplinary fluency.

title: Workscapes 2.0
description: After a well-received inaugural exhibit at SIOP 2025, Workscapes is back! Workscapes, an art exhibition curated by I-Os and for I-Os, was designed to give attendees a restorative space to pause, recharge, and connect through creative expression amid the demands of conference week. Workscapes 2.0 builds on last year's event with more viewing time, greater visibility, and enhanced interactivity, deepening its commitment to Gentle SIOP values and the belief that celebrating the whole person is what makes our SIOP community stronger.

speakers: Cong Liu | Jun Michelle Yang | Huiwen Lian | Lindsay Pyc | Oyindamola Adebo | Vi Phung
speaker_affiliations: Rutgers University · Full-time | UNC at Greensboro | Texas A&M University | McKinsey & Company | Rutgers university

description: Liu, C. (Co-Chair), Yang, J. (Co-Chair) & Lian, H. (Discussant) (2026, April 30 – May 2). Examining workplace ostracism, microaggression, and pathways to inclusiveness [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Most research on workplace ostracism and microaggression has been conducted by academic scholars. However, these subtle forms of workplace mistreatment remain pressing issues that affect organizational health and productivity. This symposium, designed for both scientists and practitioners, brings together 4 presentations from academic and industry professionals to explore subtle forms of workplace mistreatment (e.g., task ostracism, ostracism toward managers, microaggression toward female physicians) and to highlight how organizations address exclusion and subtle forms of mistreatment, and to foster company-wide inclusiveness through global employee attitude surveys.

Supporting an age-diverse workforce and facilitating retirement transitions are becoming more important for organizations. This symposium advances understanding of successful aging at work and into retirement by examining subjective age, job crafting, age-inclusive HR practices, knowledge sharing, leader uncertainty regulation behaviors, and the work–nonwork interface. Together, these studies illustrate the roles of both individual factors and organizational practices in fostering engagement, motivation, and adjustment across the later career and post-retirement journey.

description: Cheban-Gore, Y. M. (Co-Chair), Mason, L. (Co-Chair), Blair, M. D., Calderón, R., Curnow, C., Flaig, J., & Trevino, S. (2026). Safeguards or barriers? A discussion on the role of minimum qualifications [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Minimum qualifications, such as degrees, licenses and certifications, and prior experience, are intended to safeguard competence and quality. Yet these requirements may also restrict access to employment and reduce workforce diversity. This panel brings together experts from research, policy, and practice to examine the role of minimum qualifications across industries. Discussion will focus on when such requirements are essential, when they act as barriers, and how they can be aligned with skills-based approaches to hiring and workforce development.

A Second-Order Quantitative Review and Synthesis of Neuroticism and Its Correlates This symposium examines the dynamic nature of individual differences, focusing on when, why, and how individual attributes may fluctuate over time. Challenging traditional trait-based assumptions, authors highlight emerging theory and methods that capture within-person variability and contextual influences on key constructs related to self-concept, personality and cognitive schema. By integrating systematic reviews, meta-regression, and network-analytic techniques, the session advances understanding of individual differences as dynamic constructs shaped by contextual and situational dynamics.
