description: Haidar, S., McChesney, J., Moran, L., & Seybert, J. ( 2026). Community of Interest: Evaluating Wellness at Work: Balancing Evidence, Privacy, and Care. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Organizations increasingly offer wellness benefits, ranging from yoga and mindfulness to nutrition programs, as a way to boost employee well-being and organizational outcomes. Yet evaluating the effectiveness of these initiatives raises sensitive challenges around data privacy, ethics, and cultural considerations. This Community of Interest session invites practitioners and academics to share experiences, frameworks, and strategies for responsibly assessing wellness programs while balancing rigorous evidence with employee trust and care.

description: Henderson, A. A. (Chair), Ahmad, A. S., Prasad, J., Smith, C. E., & Su, S. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Assessments in an Era of AI and Evolving Technology: Panel and Workshop [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. In an era of evolving technology, educators face a challenge to ensure learning and integrity while preparing students for technology integrated workplaces. title: Do Great Teams SYNC Alike? Understanding Collective Constructs in Teams (Session ID 1429)
speakers: Janet Okoroji | Tyree Mitchell | Mikhail Wolfson | Caroline Deal | Nale Lehmann-Willenbrock
speaker_affiliations: Louisiana State University | University of Kentucky | University of South Florida | University of Hamburg

description: Okoroji, J. C. (Co-Chair), Mitchell, T. D. (Co-Chair), & Wolfson, M. (Discussant). (2026). Do Great Teams SYNC alike? Understanding Collective Constructs in Teams [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Much of the team and leadership research has relied on limited static methods to measure team interactions; however, this limits how I-Os understand the temporal development and measurement of collective constructs (e.g., affect, coordination, synchrony, and leadership). This symposium brings together 3 papers that explore understanding of team interactions and leadership through alternative approaches in methodology and theory, which can provide deeper insights on teams. The discussant will synthesize insights and provide recommendations for research and practice.

title: Taking Stock of PM: Overcoming the Past and Present for a High-Performance Future (Session ID 1421)
tracks: Job Performance/Withdrawal/Misbehavior | Content Area Theme Track
speakers: Alan Colquitt | David Futrell | Shonna Waters
speaker_affiliations: Independent Consultant | Consulting | Fractional Insights
speaker_titles: Managing Partner | CEO & Cofounder
description: Colquitt, A.; Brock, S.; Futrell, D., & Waters, S. (2026). Taking Stock of Performance Management: Overcoming the Past and Present for a High-Performance Future. Panel Discussion at the 2026 Conference for the Society of Industrial-Organizational Psychology. This panel explores why performance management (PM) and the pay-for-performance reward systems tied to it continue to fail after 70 years of research and reform. Despite constant redesign, most organizations and employees remain deeply dissatisfied with these processes. Despite this dissatisfaction, organizations have no plans to abandon their processes. Panelists will examine the flawed paradigms and practices behind PM, the forces reshaping organizations and HR practices, and how to re-imagine PM as a process that enables individual, team, and organizational performance.

title: Into the Dark: Zombie Leadership, False Weapons, and You: Our Only Hope (Session ID 1420)

Zombie leadership: Dead ideas that still walk among us [Alternative Session Type]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session will transport the audience through a cinematic journey of realization that people have been living in a zombie apocalypse. In Act I, Haslam unmasks the undead—executives who look, walk, and talk like leaders but are animated by infected practices. In Act II, Sahm cuts the lights: Executive performance data are nearly meaningless, leaving people in total darkness. Act III brings Kaiser with a plot twist— flimsy weapons are useless against the enemy. In Act IV, the audience joins the fight in a race against time to save the world from the zombie invasion.

title: No Experiment, No Problem? Causal Inference in Applied Quasi-Experimental Settings (Session ID 830)
speakers: Ludek Stehlik | Marissa Post
speaker_affiliations: Sanofi
speaker_titles: People Analytics Data Science Expert | Data Scientist, People Analytics
description: Post, M. L., Laurentsyeva, N., Stehlik, L., & Ushakov, I. (2026, April 30 – May 2). No Experiment, No Problem? Causal Inference in Applied Quasi-Experimental Settings [Master Tutorial]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Evaluating organizational programs without experiments (A/B tests) can make causal inference seem out of reach. This practice-oriented tutorial designed for a beginner to intermediate technical audience highlights usage of quasi-experimental methods and econometric/ML techniques - Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs), Generalized Estimating Equations (GEE), Coarsened Exact Matching (CEM), and Double Machine Learning (DML) - to estimate impact when randomization is infeasible. Using R and Python, participants learn to design, analyze, and communicate credible causal evidence in applied settings.

title: Approaches to Conducting Leadership Research in the Military (Session ID 574)
speakers: Ann-Louise Holten | Benjamin Elliott | Ingrid Covington | Ivana Igic
speaker_affiliations: Forsvarsakademiet · Full-time | University of Minnesota | K U Leuven | Military Academy ETH Zurich

description: Euwema, M. C. (Chair) (2026, April 30 – May 2). Approaches to Conducting Leadership Research in the Military [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Covington, I. K. & Igboanusi, G. (2026) From Isolation to Community: Inclusive Leadership in NATO. Holten, A.L, & O. H., Rahbek (2026) Political communication . Morf, M., Igic, I., Avolio, B & Inceoglu, I (2026) What Makes a Moment Matter? Rethinking Leadership Development Through the Eyes of Military Leaders. Elliott, B., Kuncel., N, (2026) Muddied Boots: Rater Source Effects in Military Academy Recommendation Letters. This symposium, Leadership Research in the Military, presents 4 complementary studies exploring contemporary leadership within complex defense contexts. Covington and Igboanusi examine an inclusive program at NATO SHAPE enhancing belonging and resilience through social identity and psychological safety. Holten and Rahbek analyze leadership as linguistic performance shaping legitimacy and collective identity. Morf, Igic, Avolio, and Inceoglu identify pivotal experiences driving leader development and adaptability. Elliott and Kuncel assess validity across leadership raters, revealing context-dependent predictors. Together, these studies redefine military leadership as adaptive, inclusive, and socially constructed.

The field of large language models (LLMs) is evolving rapidly, and many I-Os are still learning how to integrate these tools into their jobs and fully harness their potential. Many do not know where to get started on this journey. Join us for a tutorial to demystify LLMs and learn practical LLM skills. This session offers valuable insights and demonstrations for beginners and intermediates. We'll start with basics and cover many topics, including prompt engineering, using tools and Agent Mode, LLM automation, and even RAG. By the end, you'll be well equipped to integrate LLMs into your work.

title: Talent Strategy 360: Confronting Contradictions in the Future of Work (Session ID 300)

description: Gorbatov, S. (Chair), Lane, A., Effron, M., Lanik, M., Rudow, G., & Sandell, K. (2026). Talent Strategy 360: Confronting contradictions in the future of work [IGNITE]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This IGNITE session brings together 6 leading thinkers to explore the 3 essential pillars of talent strategy that organizations must diagnose and execute to thrive in the future of work: talent availability, talent quality, and talent mobility. Rather than presenting a single narrative, this session stages contrasting viewpoints on each pillar, ensuring a dynamic exploration of the paradoxes leaders face today. For each pillar, 2 speakers will offer rapid opposing perspectives: 5 minutes, 20 slides, auto-advancing. The session will conclude with an interactive Q&A.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference. New Orleans, LA, United States. Remote and hybrid work are often framed as liberating — free from commutes, rigid schedules, and in-person oversight. Yet, without clear boundaries, work often bleeds into personal life. Employees can face assumptions from others that they have endless flexibility or “aren’t really working.” During this panel, experts and practitioners will discuss remote work through self-regulation, gendered stereotypes, and work–life boundary management. Participants will leave with insights and practical tools to help themselves and their organizations navigate hidden constraints of working from home.

With the professionalization of collegiate athletes, record-high valuations of sports franchises, the globalization of sports, and advancements in analytics and talent evaluation, sports organizations have been called to transform their talent management practices. Although I-O research in this context is sparse, the field is well-positioned to offer scientific, strategic, people-centered approaches to selecting and developing talent. To extend research, authors present papers on elite athletes, emphasizing typical yet critical I-O constructs for predicting behavior and performance on the job.

Visual-spatial ability is the capacity to generate, retain, retrieve, and transform spatial information as well as structured visual images. Spatial ability carries importance for a wide range of occupations. However, personnel selection researchers have largely neglected spatial ability and its measurement. This symposium invites modern spatial and visual ability researchers and practitioners to explore innovative methodologies and emergent technologies that expand the understanding and application of spatial ability assessments.

title: The 2% Problem: Cultivating Sustainable High Performance Systems at Scale (Session ID 1298)
tracks: Job Performance/Withdrawal/Misbehavior | Content Area Theme Track
speakers: Kristen Williamson | Cat Yaris | Cathy Maraist | Laura Joiner
speaker_affiliations: Spencer Stuart | Capital One | Cencora | Demandbase

description: Williamson, K. S. (Chair), Yaris, C., & Joiner, L., Maraist, C.., Wilken, H., (2026, April 30- May 2). The 2% Problem: Cultivating Sustainable High Performance Systems at Scale [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, L.A., United States. The focus on "star" performers is challenged by research showing performance is cyclical and context-dependent. This exposes a "knowing-doing gap": research has shown many ingredients for a high-performance culture, but practitioners still struggle with workable recipes. This panel confronts this by exploring & discussing 3 practitioner dilemmas: the complexity of integration, the nuances of measurement, and the challenge of sustainability. The session provides actionable strategies for building performance systems that are not only effective but truly enduring.

description: Jeong, S. (Co-Chair), & Chang, C. H. D. (Co-Chair) (2026, April 30 – May 2). It takes two: Provider and recipient perspective on workplace helping [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Magalona, J., Blocker, V., & Ryan, A. M. (2026). Performative vs. genuine helping behaviors: Insights from men’s allyship. Jeong, S., Keller, A. C., Chang, C. H. D., Chong, S., & Bryant, C. (2026). When help hurts: How social burden affect provider well-being through basic needs. Baker, N. (2026). Unhelpful Support: Threats to basic needs predict negative evaluations of help. Helping behavior at work is not uniformly beneficial; its effects depend on the motivations, interpretations, and experiences of both helpers and recipients. This symposium includes 3 papers that examine the complex exchanges between providers and recipients of help. Outcomes for help recipients (e.g., help acceptance, perceived help quality) and help providers (e.g., motivations for and barriers to helping, work engagement, meaningfulness, exhaustion) are examined using various study designs, including qualitative interviews, experimental vignette studies, and daily diary surveys.

Debating the Future of Employee Listening [IGNITE]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. The debate over employee surveys is intensifying as organizations seek faster, less intrusive ways to understand their workforce. In this IGNITE! session, 6 presenters will cover a range of perspectives challenging the future of both traditional survey methods and emerging passive, AI-enabled technologies. Time will be held at the end for further debate and audience Q&A. The goal is to walk away with (1) a more nuanced understanding of where critical disruption is – or is not – needed alongside (2) practical ideas for utilizing the best of both traditional and emerging methods.

Bringing decent work to the next generation of I-O Professionals (Panel discussion). Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Professors from 5 universities and 3 nations will discuss how they address decent work and methods they employ to prepare future generations to improve workers’ well-being. Decent work was first advocated by the International Labor Organization and is now a part of the UN’s sustainable Development Goals. Decent work is productive, delivers fair income, security, social protection, chances for development, freedom of expression, the right to organize, and equal opportunity for women and men. It is congruent with the I-O principles but is rarely in textbooks or taught to students.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States This panel examines the application of artificial intelligence (AI) to streamline Job Task Analysis (JTA) processes. AI can cluster job roles based on JTA data, facilitate skills-based competency modeling, and inform various practices. title: Beyond the Glass Ceiling: Dominance Stereotypes and Spillover Barriers for Women Lead (Session ID 1344)
speakers: Pratibha Deepak | Feng Guo | Abigail Folberg | Charlotte Moser | Daphna Motro
speaker_affiliations: University Of Tennessee Chattanooga | University of Tennessee At Chattanooga | University of Nebraska - Omaha | University of Tennessee at Chattanooga | Hofstra University
speaker_titles: Assistant Professor | Associate Professor
description: Deepak, P (Chair), (2026, April 30 – May 2). Beyond the Glass Ceiling: Dominance Stereotypes and Spillover Barriers for Women Leaders [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Women face unique barriers after reaching leadership. This symposium integrates 4 studies examining how dominance stereotypes, stigma spillover, and identity threats restrict women's progress in leadership roles. Authors investigate which stereotype dimensions constrain representation, how individual failures produce group penalties, and which organizational interventions work. Findings reveal dominance (not competence) predicts bias, one woman's failure affects others' opportunities, public failures trigger guilt-by-association, and visible male allyship outperforms diversity statements.

Return-to-office (RTO) decisions are as much about communication as policy. This panel explores how the framing of RTO — whether as a loss of flexibility, a path to culture, or a business necessity — shapes employee sentiment, trust, and engagement. Internal practitioners share lessons from their organizations, and an external researcher offers workforce data and client insights. The discussion highlights timely, evidence-based guidance for leaders navigating hybrid work.

title: “Meaning In” Work or Life Within the Work Stress Framework (Session ID 607)
speakers: Marcus Fila | Sharon Glazer | Brent Reed | Norbert Semmer | Peter McEachern
speaker_affiliations: Hope College | Rutgers, The State University of NJ | University of North Carolina at Charlotte | University of Bern, Switzerland | George Mason University
speaker_titles: Associate Professor Of Management | Assistant Professor, Psychological and Organizational Science | Professor Emeritus | Graduate Teaching Assistant
description: Fila, M. J. (Co-Chair), & Glazer, S. (Co-Chair). (2026, April 30-May 2). ‘Meaning in’ work or life within the work stress framework [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. There is increasing attention in the media and industry reports on the notion of purpose and meaning. Despite the topic not being new, research within the work domain remains limited, particularly with respect to work stress. In response, this symposium showcases new empirical and theoretical research on the role of meaning in work and life in the experience of stressors and strains at work. In particular, the presentations explore meaning as an appraisal orientation or a process variable that has protective qualities to thwart stressors and strains. This session offers relevant, fresh perspectives for scholars and practitioners alike.

description: Kalafut, C., Ko, I., Parker, B., Shah, M., & Thornton, J. (2026). Human strategy or tech infrastructure? A structured debate on people analytics’ future [Debate]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. People analytics is racing ahead on 2 tracks: one pulled by rapidly evolving tech stacks (cloud data platforms, AI/ML, self-serve BI), the other by human-centered strategy (I-O rigor, EX design, change and ethics). This session stages a fast-paced debate where panelists rotate positions on core prompts (e.g., org placement, build-vs-buy, skills mix, and governance). Authors pressure-test when technology intensity creates scale without impact—and when strategy depth wins influence but hits capacity limits.

description: Moustakis, K., Bank, J., and Weintraub, J. (2026, April 30 - May 2). Beyond the Niche: I-Os’ Fight for Business Relevance [Alternative Session]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Tech, financial services, consumer products, and healthcare comprise over 70% of S&P 500 value, yet less than 10% of SIOP members indicate working in these areas, suggesting underrepresentation in key industries. As such, panelists with unusual roles, such as founder coaching, GenAI adoption at Deloitte, and sales operations at MongoDB and Uber, will share how they reposition I-Os from being critical of business to more business critical. (2026, April). 360 surveys: Stuck in time, or ready to revolutionize the future of talent? [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This practitioner-led panel revisits SIOP’s 2011 debate: Have 360s truly evolved or are they stuck in time? As technology, talent demands, and AI reshape how feedback is gathered and used, the panel will explore what’s changed, what hasn’t, and what the future holds. Grounded in recent industry benchmarking, this lively discussion will examine purpose, best practices, and the ethical use of AI to navigate the opportunities and risks shaping the future of 360 feedback.

This session examines both the theoretical implications and practical challenges of AI's impact on validity theory and assessment practice. title: Where AI Stops and Human Responsibility Starts in Employee Experience (Session ID 1328)
speakers: Kamela Lupino | Ali Badibanga | Cat Yaris | Elizabeth Conjar | Jes Beck | Matthew Evans | Nikola Hartling

description: Lupino, K., Evans, M., O’Kain, A., Hartling, N., Badibanga, A., Conjar, E., Beck, J., Hartling, N., Yaris, C. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Where AI Stops and Human Responsibility Starts in Employee Experience [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Although AI has enabled employee experience (EX) insights to be delivered faster and at scale, AI alone does not create a consistent EX nor its desired outcomes (productivity, retention, etc.). In fact, handled poorly, AI has the ability to harm the quality and impact of actioning. Geared toward intermediate practitioners and drawing on the latest research, this panel, which includes real-world application from organization EX leaders, will discuss how and where to harness the capabilities AI offers, and where human touch remains essential.

speakers: Debra Comer | Ludmila Praslova

description: Praslova, L., & Comer, D. R. (2026, April 30 – May 2). From Harm to Dignity: Ethical Foundations for Autism at Work Research and Application [Master Tutorial]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. I-O psychologists are uniquely positioned to do tremendous good in their autism at work research and practice. However, traditional autism frameworks rooted in clinical paradigms can cause unintended harm when applied without critical examination. This tutorial provides a practical framework for designing research and applications that honor autistic people's worth, voice, and expertise. Through case analysis examining both what goes wrong and what works well, participants would develop skills to recognize dignity risks and create work environments that enable autistic people to thrive.

title: Eldercare in Organizations: Employee, Couple, and Caregiver Perspectives (Session ID 611)

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Schirle, G., Peng, Y., Zhan, J., & Zhang, W. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Taullahu, D.B., Smith, C.E., Allen, T.D., & Carrizalez, D.D. (2026, April 30 – May 2). A Meta-Ethnography of Stress in the Caregiving Workforce: A Challenge-Hinderance Perspective. This symposium brings together 3 distinct but complementary perspectives on how employees navigate these demands: (a) employees providing informal care to aging relatives, (b) dual-earner couples managing eldercare responsibilities, and (c) formal caregivers employed in paid eldercare roles. Across these works, authors highlight both the challenges and opportunities of eldercare, examining how individual, relational, and organizational factors shape outcomes such as thriving, relationship quality, and well-being. Together, the papers underscore the importance of understanding eldercare as a multifaceted work–family issue with significant implications for organizations, employees, and families.

title: Climbers Versus Performers: Disentangling Executive Confidence From Competence (Session ID 249)
speakers: Reece Akhtar | Ted Hayes | Mina Morris
speaker_affiliations: Deeper Signals | Consulting Psychology Journal: Practice and Research | Accenture
speaker_titles: CEO, co-founder | Editor in Chief | Head of Assessments
description: Akhtar, R., Morris, M., & Hayes, T. (2026, April 30–May 2). Climbers vs. performers: Disentangling executive confidence from competence [Panel discussion]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Many executives labeled “high potential” rise quickly due to confidence and ambition—traits that predict emergence but not long-term success. This panel examines the bias favoring “climbers” over true “performers” with data from global employers, showing how judgment and learning agility better predict effectiveness. Authors share evidence-based assessment and coaching practices, including AI-enabled approaches, to help organizations reward competence rather than confidence.

(2026, April 30 – May 2) Daily Leadership: Coping Methods and the Role of Regret and Happiness A recent report estimated that workplace mistreatment costs organizations over $914 billion in the United States alone. Although workplace mistreatment includes bullying, harassment, incivility, and abusive supervision, scholars argue that workplace mistreatment cannot be understood through isolated perspectives. The juggle is real: The nature and consequences of profiles of identity management for working parents. Working parenthood is not a uniform experience but is shaped by diverse, intersecting identities and varied strategies parents use to manage them. This symposium brings together 4 papers that advance understanding of these complexities: 2 papers examine parenthood through an intersectional lens, and 2 focus on identity management processes in the workplace. Together, this symposium advances understanding of the diverse realities of parenthood at work, and offers practical insights on how organizations can better support and retain working parents across diverse identities. Dr. Winny Shen will serve as the discussant, providing an integrative commentary.

title: Blurring Boundaries: Advances in Theory and Research on the Work–Home Interface (Session ID 284)
speakers: Arnold Bakker | Evangelia Demerouti | Alyssa Lezcano
speaker_affiliations: Erasmus University Rotterdam | Eindhoven University of Technology | University of South Florida
speaker_titles: Professor | Doctoral Candidate
description: Bakker, A. B., & Demerouti, E. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Blurring boundaries: Advances in theory and research on the work–home interface [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. As work and home boundaries blur, understanding their interplay is crucial for sustainable well-being and performance. This symposium presents new insights into the work–home interface. Hammer et al. show that sustained supervisor and coworker support reduces work–family conflict. Lezcano et al. reveal how dual-earner couples jointly manage boundaries under remote work. Demerouti et al. find that family–work facilitation enhances engagement and creativity, whereas job crafting buffers conflict effects. Bakker et al. demonstrate that leisure crafting replenishes energy when family demands are high.

Annual Conference of the Society of Industrial and Organizational Psychology, New Orleans, LA, United States. This session explores the challenges of implementing 360-degree feedback across different cultures and languages in global organizations. Drawing on data from major multinational companies, authors examine the magnitude of cultural differences, whether they reflect genuine behavioral variations or biases, and discuss best practices for equitable comparisons through audience participation. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, United States. Su, R., Luo, J., & Cooney, R. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Underemployment and Personality Change: Insights from Two National Longitudinal Studies. Sylvara, A., Sun, T., & Luo, J. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Influences of work characteristics on personality trait adaptation and reciprocal relationships. Xi, M., Sun, T., Sun, J., Wu, W., Jackson, J. J. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Person-Team (PT) Fit: Dynamic Processing of Inter-personal Behavior and Job Performance. In organizational research, personality has been predominantly approached from a dispositional perspective, treating personality as a static and relatively stable predictor of work-related outcomes. However, recent research positions personality as more dynamic and changes in tandem with work-related experiences. This symposium presents 4 studies that examine personality change and dynamics within organizational contexts, offering several insights on personality change related to underemployment, job characteristics, turnover, and person-team fit.

description: Sessa, V.I. (Co-Chair) & Su, S. (Co-Chair) (2026, April 30 – May 2). Cutting through the jangle: A review of methods for addressing construct proliferation [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Construct proliferation is a common problem across many fields, including industrial and organizational psychology. Although construct proliferation is a recognized problem, relatively few methods have been applied to assess it comprehensively. The purpose of this session is to showcase a variety of traditional and innovative methods that can be used to determine if 2 or more constructs are redundant. Although the presenters draw on their own research topics (team cognitions, job attitudes, leadership, and workplace aggression), these will serve as the “ground” while the focus will be on the methods used.

description: Vergauwe, J. (Chair) & Harms, P. (Discussant) (2026, April 30 – May 2). Dark Traits in Leadership: From Situational Triggers to Follower Outcomes [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Dark traits such as narcissism, Machiavellianism, and psychopathy are a common cause of derailment in leaders, but their effects are not uniformly destructive. This symposium showcases research that moves beyond static views on leaders’ dark traits to examine their dynamic expression, situational triggers, and effects on follower and team outcomes. Spanning within-person, dyadic, and team-level analyses, the papers reveal when and how dark traits harm or help. An expert discussant integrates insights, offering a more nuanced perspective on dark leadership.

title: Unsupervised Machine Learning for I-O Psychology Research: Anomaly Detection (Session ID1081)
speakers: Abhishek Srivastava
speaker_affiliations: Ball State University
speaker_titles: Professor and Department Chair
description: Srivastava, A. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Unsupervised Machine Learning for I-O Psychology Research: Anomaly Detection [Master Tutorial]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Labeling the data for training and testing purposes (as in supervised machine learning) may not be feasible in some cases. In this tutorial, I will discuss unsupervised machine learning techniques that do not require labeled data and result in an automated classification of observations or grouping of variables for further analysis. I will focus on a type of density estimation technique called anomaly detection. I will illustrate the technique through a publicly available or a valid AI-generated Python code and synthetic/simulated dataset with numeric (not text) variables.

speakers: Yizhen Egyn Zhu | Yi Wang | Michael Campion | Hanna Hlebasko | Jingyi Li | Yuyun Huang

description: Wang, Y., & Zhu, Y. E. (Co-Chairs) (2026). Responsible AI in IO research and practice: Examples and lessons. [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. As I-O psychologists increasingly adopt Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Large Language Models (LLM), the stakes have never been higher for the responsible use of AI in I-O research and practices. This symposium shares examples and lessons for implementing responsible AI in I-O research and practices, including scoring narrative responses, automated interviewer debrief preparation process, and competency modeling. The studies use a variety of methods to enhance responsible AI usage, such as Chain-of-Thoughts prompting, input and output sanitization, LLM-as-Judge, and human-in-the-loop.

description: Maindidze, H. T. (Co-chair), Randall, J. G. (Co-chair), & Ahmad, A. S. (Discussant). (2026). Advancements in Diversity Training Research and Practice. [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. The science and practice of diversity training is facing existential challenges. In response, the papers included in this symposium each present something new, including (a) a mindfulness-based diversity training, (b) evidence-based and culturally responsive training design, (c) employee motivations to attend diversity training, and (d) exploring dissenting diversity attitudes in the post-training environment. Authors hope attendees will come with open minds and critical questions to help chart a new path for the future of diversity training.

title: SIOP Special Session: Toxic CULT-Ure: Why Don’t You Just Quit? (Session ID 1445)
speakers: Adam Smith | Brian Ruggeberg | Dominic Fedele | Nicole Voss | Nikita Mikhailov | Liana Kreamer
speaker_affiliations: Harvard University | Spencer Stuart | Florida Institute of Technology | VF Corporation | SIOP

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Leaving a toxic workplace is rarely simple. Employees often see the dysfunction yet stay—held by identity, belonging, fear, or hope things will improve. This panel explores why toxic cultures feel “sticky,” examining factors like sunk-cost thinking, subtle rewards, retaliation fears, and personal circumstances. Authors discuss how workplace rituals and narratives normalize harm and spill into personal life. Participants will gain research-based insights and strategies for recognizing, escaping, and preventing toxic CULT-ure.

Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. When an employee’s dignity is assaulted in the workplace and accountability never comes, how do they carry on, when leaving isn’t an option? This session explores how individuals resist and restore as acts of maintaining and strengthening dignity, reclaiming agency and meaning in the face of silence or disregard. The interdisciplinary panel of scholars and practitioners blends research and practice to illuminate how people rise after dignity assaults and practical approaches to supporting these employees.

Society of Industrial and Organizational Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Organizations face 2 simultaneous demographic shifts: unpredictable late-career retirement decisions and the presence of 5 generations working together. These transitions affect knowledge transfer, succession planning, collaboration, workplace culture, and evolving career expectations. This Community of Interest will spark conversation on how organizations can adapt to these dynamics, foster intergenerational learning, build stability amid uncertainty, and design work that supports both seasoned employees and emerging talent.

Changes in procedural justice perceptions of affirmative action hiring plans. Recent attacks on workplace diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) have changed the landscape of both research and practice. As many DEI efforts are currently being reconsidered, rescinded, or renamed, understanding how attitudes toward DEI efforts develop is increasingly important. This symposium showcases research aimed at understanding how organizational DEI policies are perceived throughout the employment lifecycle, from prospective applicants to labor market participants. In doing so, authors shed light on how DEI messaging impacts perceivers and why some remain resistant to DEI policies.

title: Rethinking Emotion Regulation: Context, Automaticity, and Computational Modeling (Session ID 696)
tracks: Motivation, Attitudes, and Emotions | Content Area Theme Track

description: Gallegos, E., (Co-Chair) Diefendorff, J. M., (Co-Chair) & Kenworthy, J. B. (Discussant) (2026, April 30 – May 2). Rethinking emotion regulation: Context, automaticity, and computational modeling [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. From job interviews to workplace interactions, understanding how people regulate emotions in modern work requires new perspectives and tools. This symposium highlights 3 studies that offer fresh insights into emotion regulation research: the first examines deep and surface acting in interviews, the second reconceptualizes emotion regulation as an automatic rather than solely effortful process, and the third presents a use case of interpersonal emotion coregulation as a foundation for computational modeling to advance emotion regulation theory and research.

Leveraging AI for efficient assessment scoring optimization through AutoML Assessment development often requires considerable input from subject matter experts (SMEs), which can draw out the development process and increase costs. Society of Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Floyd, T.M., Woehler, M., Cullen-Lester, K., Yang, S.W., & Yu, S. (2026, April 30-May 2). Networking Up: How Targeting Superiors vs. Peers Affects Observers’ Inferences about Networkers’ Motives. Woehler, M., Cullen-Lester, K.L., Porter, C.M., & Gerbasi, A. (2026, April 30-May 2). Should I Reach Up? Why and When Higher-Ranking Contacts Differentially Benefit Men's and Women’s Performance Evaluations. Lee, A.K. (2026, April 30-May 2). Perceived Relational Energy Comparison: Reframing Leader Energy as a Socially Constructed Signal. Choudhury, A. & Maupin, C.K. (2026, April 30-May 2). Signal Consistency in Shaping Team Processes: The Roles of LMX Differentiation and Reward Interdependence. Employees are often encouraged to “reach up” to higher-ranking colleagues, yet such ties send powerful social signals that shape how others interpret motives, fairness, and status. This symposium examines how upward relationships are perceived and their outcomes. Four papers explore networking motives, gendered interpretations of higher-ranking contacts, comparative perceptions of leader energy, and the alignment of leader–member exchange with reward systems. Together, they show that upward ties are not neutral resources but visible cues shaping meaning, inclusion, and cooperation at work.

As AI technologies become integrated into organizational systems and workflows, there is a urgent need to address the central role of humans in shaping sustainable, ethical, and high-performing workplaces. This SIOP 2026 panel will focus on the necessity of applying “People Science” as a critical part of AI integration and digital disruption. Drawing from research on human performance, organizational behavior, and technological change, the session highlights how scholars, practitioners, consultants, and technology vendors can work together to unlock human potential in an AI-enabled workplace.

title: Challenges to Physical Performance Assessment in 2026 (Session ID 1386)
speakers: Deborah Gebhardt | Todd Baker
speaker_affiliations: HumRRO
speaker_titles: Chief Scientist
description: Gebhardt, D. L. (Chair) (2026, April 30 – May 2). Challenges to physical assessment in 2026 [Symposium]. Society of Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Dulla, J. J. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Physiological testing trends: Demographic, fitness, and technological challenges. Gebhardt, D. L. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Fit for physical work or fit for fairness? Job simulations versus normed scoring. Baker, T. A. ((2026, April 30 – May 2). Physical Testing Litigation, Remote Testing, and the Role of AI. Physical performance assessment in 2026 has become more challenging due to demographic shifts and the varied court rulings. This symposium addresses age- and sex-normed scoring for basic ability tests and implications for hiring qualified individuals. With the increase in gender identities, difficulties related to normed scoring tend to be addressed using a job simulation approach. Finally, advances in smartphone technology and AI in the testing process are discussed, along with how they can support an applicant mentor process.

title: AI, Fairness, and the Future of Personality Testing (Session ID 961)
speakers: Tariq Shaban | Deniz Ones | Heather Myers | Kayden Stockdale
speaker_affiliations: Podium | University of Minnesota · Full-time | Traitify by Crosschq | Virginia Tech
speaker_titles: Director of Partner Success | Distinguished McKnight University Prof.; Hellervik Prof. of Industrial Psychology | Chief Scientific Officer | PhD Student
description: Shaban, T. (Chair), (2026, April 30 – May 2). AI, Fairness, and the Future of Personality Testing: Balancing Fairness, Engagement, and Validity in Personality Testing [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Advances in artificial intelligence (AI) and computational psychometrics are transforming personality assessment to be more adaptive, fair, and engaging. This symposium highlights 3 innovations: image-based assessments that enhance accessibility and candidate experience; large language models (LLMs) that audit fairness by detecting semantic and cultural bias; and AI-driven adaptive testing using non-linear, forced-choice models to reduce faking and improve validity. A closing commentary examines the ethical and theoretical implications of AI-enabled assessment for transparency and responsible practice. Together, these advances show how AI can enhance psychometric rigor and fairness in modern testing.

speakers: Tracy Kantrowitz | Caribay Garcia

description: Demko, B., Kane, L., & Kantrowitz, T. (2026). Assessment in the Flow of Work: Charting a Course for the Future of Assessment [Panel Discussion]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. Enterprises are shifting toward skills-based talent management, creating demand for scalable, objective skill assessment. Traditional testing cannot support the vast number of skills required. Emerging GenAI approaches offer potential to extract skill signals from everyday work artifacts, yet their validity and fairness remain untested. This session explores research using large language models to evaluate real work products, comparing AI and human ratings to assess feasibility, reliability, and implications for the future of assessment.

title: Building Future-Ready Leaders in the AI Era: Implications for Leadership Development (Session ID 414)
speakers: Amanda Julian | Beau River | Jean-Simon Leclerc | Katherine Gibbard
speaker_affiliations: Monark | Leadership Delta Partners | SuccessFinder | SAP SuccessFactors
speaker_titles: Co-Founder & Chief Science Officer | Founder + Managing Partner | Vice-President | Client Success and R&D | Senior Research Scientist
description: Lacerenza, C.N. (Chair), Leclerc, J.S., Julian, A., Gibbard, K., River, B.. Building Future-Ready Leaders in the AI Era: Implications for Leadership Development [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, Louisiana, United States. This panel explores the evolving nature of leadership, focusing on the role of talent data in generating leadership insights, and how AI is transforming the landscape of leadership “intelligence.” What data matters most, and how can it be used responsibly and effectively? Finally, authors discuss the evolving partnership between human practitioners and AI as well as the role that I-O psychology science should play in shaping the future of modern leadership development.

title: Connecting the Dots: Network Perspectives on Organizational Attitudes and Behavior (Session ID 1017)
speakers: Gary Burns | Steven Holthouser | Nathan Carter | Ryan Thibodeau | Alexander DeChurch
speaker_affiliations: Florida Institute of Technology | Northrop Grumman | PepsiCo
speaker_titles: Professor | People Insights Analyst
description: Burns, G. N. (Co-Chair), Holthouser, S. A. (Co-Chair), & Carter, N. T. (Discussant). (2026, April 30 – May 2). Connecting the dots: Network perspectives on organizational attitudes and behavior [Symposium]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This symposium highlights how network analysis offers a unifying framework for understanding organizational attitudes and relationships. Using both psychometric and egocentric approaches, 3 studies examine organizational commitment, newcomer socialization, and training evaluation through the lens of interconnected systems. Together, they demonstrate how mapping these networks reveals the central links that shape behavior, engagement, and organizational outcomes.

title: AI-Driven Work Analysis: Balancing Automation and Human Insight in Talent Management (Session ID 1140)
speakers: Ren Nygren | Dr. Ashley A. Walvoord | Julie Lyon | Melanie Standish | Yuyan (Zoe) Zhang
speaker_affiliations: APTMetrics | Talent and AI Executive Leader | LinkedIn | Lenovo
speaker_titles: Chief Operating Officer | Sr. Manager, Talent Architecture | Senior Leadership & Organization Development Specialist
description: Nygren, R. (Chair), Lyon, J., Standish, M., Walvoord, A., & Zhang, Y. (2026). AI-driven work analysis: Balancing automation and human insight in talent management [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel examines AI-driven work analysis tools that promise to streamline work analysis by parsing job descriptions, postings, and internal data. Drawing on field research and case studies, panelists compare outputs generated by AI alone versus AI reviewed by human experts, assessing their accuracy and usefulness across different talent management processes. Authors discuss best practices, risks, and guidelines for integrating AI with human expertise in work analysis.

title: Has GenAI Taken My Job Yet? I-O’s Role in the GenAI Workplace (Session ID 698)
speakers: Robert Brusso | Ann Kwak | Daniel Schmerling | Anne Scaduto
speaker_affiliations: Amazon | Capital One | GE Aerospace
speaker_titles: Senior Research Scientist | Sr People Scientist, Talent Strategy & Analytics | Senior Applied Scientist | Talent Assessment Leader
description: Brusso, R. C. (Chair), Kwak, A., Scaduto, A., & Schmerling, D. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Has GenAI taken my job yet? What’s changed for I-Os with the emergence of GenAI in the workplace [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. As GenAI continues to reshape the workplace, I-O psychologists face pressing questions about their professional future. This panel brings together industry experts to demonstrate how GenAI serves as a powerful tool to enhance rather than replace I-O capabilities. Through real-world examples, panelists will showcase the critical intersection of human expertise and AI capabilities, highlighting areas where I-O psychology expertise remains irreplaceable. Attendees will gain practical insights into effectively leveraging GenAI while maintaining core I-O competencies.

title: Big Feedback Energy: Bold Coaching for Bold Personalities (Session ID 465)
speakers: Alise Dabdoub | Erin Eason | Kristy Busija | Marina Mery | Mark Shoemaker | Marlene Dunne
speaker_affiliations: Hogan Assessments | The People Concept | Next Conversation Consulting | SKS Consulting | SHL
speaker_titles: Director of Product Innovation | President, Executive Coach | CEO | Talent Assessment Consultant | Expert Advisor
description: Dabdoub, A. (Chair), Shoemaker, M., Mery, M., Busija, K., Dunne, M., & Eason, E. (2026, April 30 – May 2). Big Feedback Energy: Bold Coaching for Bold Personalities [Panel]. Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology Annual Conference, New Orleans, LA, United States. This panel offers practical strategies for coaching leaders with emotional volatility or overconfidence traits that can distort self-awareness and hinder growth. Drawing on research linking personality traits to 360-feedback gaps, panelists will share evidence-based techniques for delivering tough feedback, fostering emotional regulation, building humility, and sustaining change. Attendees will gain tools to support bold leaders in becoming more self-aware, resilient, and effective.
